attractions.name: Acme Oyster House
attractions.description: Acme Oyster House is a timeless New Orleans establishment that has been shucking oysters and serving hungry diners since 1910. Located just off Bourbon Street in the French Quarter, it offers a vintage slice of New Orleans life in a casual, down-home atmosphere. The menu features classic Cajun and Creole fare, including their world-famous chargrilled oysters, po' boys, and seafood gumbo
attractions.address: 724 Iberville St, New Orleans, LA 70130, USA
attractions.hours: Monday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Tuesday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Wednesday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Thursday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Friday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Saturday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Sunday: 11:00 AM - 10:00 PM
attractions.days_open: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Varies (Menu items range from ~$5.49 for soup to ~$32.99 for large platters; Oysters are at Market Price)
highlights: World-famous Chargrilled Oysters saturated in herb butter and cheese
Acme \"10 Napkin\" Roast Beef Po-boy with slow-cooked chuck roast
New Orleans Medley: a sampling of gumbo, jambalaya, and red beans & rice
Fresh, ice-cold raw oysters shucked to order at the bar
Fried Peace Maker Po-boy featuring golden fried oysters and shrimp with Tabasco mayo
Authentic atmosphere with a long history dating back to 1910
attractions.tips: Acme does not accept reservations, so arrive early to avoid the often long lines that wrap around the corner. For a unique experience, try to snag a seat at the oyster bar to watch the shuckers in action. If you're looking to avoid the wait, visiting during mid-afternoon (around 2-3 PM) on a weekday is often recommended. Be prepared for a lively, sometimes loud, and authentic NOLA dining environment
attractions.nearby: 21st Amendment Bar at La Louisiane, Jester Mardi Gras Daiquiris, Bourbon Street, Hotel Monteleone, Carousel Bar, Olde Nola Cookery, GW Fins

attractions.name: Antoine's Restaurant
attractions.description: Established in 1840, Antoine's Restaurant is the oldest continually family-owned and operated restaurant in the United States. Located in the heart of the French Quarter, it is world-renowned for its fine French-Creole cuisine and historic atmosphere. The restaurant features 15 unique dining rooms and has hosted numerous celebrities, presidents, and royalty over its 180-year history
attractions.address: 713 Saint Louis Street, New Orleans, LA 70130
attractions.hours: Dining Room: Dinner (Mon-Sun) 5:00pm-9:00pm; Lunch (Mon, Thu, Fri) 10:30am-2:00pm; Brunch (Sat-Sun) 10:30am-2:00pm. Hermes Bar: Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat 10:30am-close; Tue-Wed 4:00pm-close; Sun 10:30am-2:00pm
attractions.admission: Varies (a la carte dining; lunch special around $26)
highlights: Birthplace of Oysters Rockefeller, invented by Jules Alciatore in 1899
Features 15 themed dining rooms including the Rex, Proteus, and 1840 rooms
Historic "Wine Alley" holding approximately 25,000 bottles of wine
Famous original dishes such as Eggs Sardou and Pommes de Terre Souffles
Oldest family-run restaurant in the United States, managed by the 5th generation
Sunday Jazz Brunch featuring live music and classic Creole favorites
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended; a strict business casual dress code is enforced (jackets suggested for gentlemen). Be sure to take a stroll to the back to see the historic dining rooms and the wine alley window on Royal Street
attractions.nearby: Brennan's Restaurant, Napoleon House, Beauregard-Keyes House, Preservation Hall, Jackson Square

attractions.name: Arnaud's French 75 Bar
attractions.description: Arnaud's French 75 Bar is an award-winning cocktail lounge located in the French Quarter within the historic Arnaud's Restaurant complex. It is celebrated for its turn-of-the-century elegance, white-jacketed service, and a cocktail program that earned a James Beard Award. The bar focuses on premium spirits and classic New Orleans drinks, most notably its signature cognac-based French 75
attractions.hours: Monday – Thursday: 5:30 PM – 10:30 PM; Friday: 11:30 AM – 1:30 PM (Lunch), 5:30 PM – 10:30 PM; Saturday: 5:30 PM – 10:30 PM; Sunday: 10:00 AM – 2:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: James Beard Award winner for Outstanding Bar Program
Signature French 75 cocktail made with Cognac
Vintage custom-built bar and bar-back from the late 1800s
Sunday Jazz Brunch featuring a limited a la carte menu
Upscale bar menu with classics like Soufflé Potatoes and Shrimp Remoulade
Located inside the landmark 1918 Arnaud's Restaurant complex
attractions.tips: Arrive early as the space is intimate and seating is limited; it is walk-in only and fills up quickly. Note the dress code: no T-shirts, athletic wear, or flip flops are permitted. Be sure to visit the Germaine Wells Mardi Gras Museum located upstairs in the restaurant for a free look at vintage Carnival costumes
attractions.nearby: Bourbon Street, Royal Street antique shops, Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, Old Absinthe House

attractions.name: Arnaud's Restaurant
attractions.description: Arnaud's is a historic, upscale Creole restaurant in the heart of New Orleans' French Quarter, originally opened in 1918. Now operated by the fourth generation of the Casbarian family, it features 17 opulent dining rooms, including a lively Jazz Bistro and the award-winning French 75 Bar. The establishment is renowned for its classic Creole cuisine, handcrafted cocktails, and an on-site museum dedicated to Mardi Gras history
attractions.hours: Dinner: Monday – Saturday, 5:30pm – 9:00pm; Friday Lunch: 11:30am – 1:30pm; Sunday Jazz Brunch: 10:00am – 1:30pm
attractions.admission: Varies (Prix fixe and à la carte options available); Free admission to the on-site Mardi Gras Museum
highlights: Historic Main Dining Room with crystal chandeliers and marble floors
Germaine Cazenave Wells Mardi Gras Museum featuring vintage costumes and masks
Award-winning French 75 Bar known for classic cocktails and fine cigars
Sunday Jazz Brunch featuring a live strolling jazz trio
Signature Creole dishes like Shrimp Arnaud and Bananas Foster
Extensive private dining options across 17 historic rooms
attractions.tips: A strict dress code is enforced: jackets are preferred and collared shirts are required for gentlemen in the main dining room, while shorts, t-shirts, and flip-flops are prohibited. Reservations are highly recommended, especially for the popular Sunday Jazz Brunch. The on-site Mardi Gras Museum is free and open to the public during restaurant hours
attractions.nearby: Bourbon Street, Sazerac House, Carousel Bar at Hotel Monteleone, St. Louis Cathedral, Jackson Square

attractions.name: Audubon Aquarium (formerly Audubon Aquarium of the Americas)
attractions.description: The Audubon Aquarium, located along the Mississippi River, features over 3,600 animals across 250 species. Following a major renovation in 2023, it now shares its building with the Audubon Insectarium, offering an immersive look at aquatic life and insects. Highlights include the walk-through Maya Tunnel, the Gulf of Mexico exhibit, and a playful colony of African penguins
attractions.hours: Daily: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Aquarium & Insectarium: Adult $45–$55, Child/Senior $40–$50; Zoo: Adult $30–$40, Child/Senior $25–$35; Combo (All 3): Adult $60, Child/Senior $55; Children under 2: Free
highlights: Maya Tunnel: A walk-through tunnel surrounded by ocean life
Gulf of Mexico Exhibit: A 400,000-gallon tank featuring sharks, sea turtles, and rays
African Penguins: A breeding colony of endangered South African penguins
Shark Discovery: A 13,000-gallon touch pool for interacting with sharks and rays
Down the Bayou: An exhibit featuring Louisiana wetlands wildlife, including a rare white alligator
Amazon Rainforest: A lush gallery with free-flying birds and tropical species like the two-toed sloth
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets online in advance to skip lines. The Aquarium and Insectarium are now in the same building, so one ticket covers both. Arrive early at 10 AM to avoid crowds, and consider the combo ticket if you plan to visit the Zoo as well. Check the schedule for animal feedings, such as the stingrays at 1:30 PM
attractions.nearby: Audubon Insectarium (same building), Woldenberg Park, French Quarter, Harrah's Casino, Canal Street Ferry, The Outlet Collection at Riverwalk

attractions.name: Bar Tonique
attractions.description: Bar Tonique is a cozy, brick-walled craft cocktail bar located on the edge of the French Quarter, known for its "swanky dive" vibe and innovative libations. Since opening in 2008, it has focused on serving expertly crafted cocktails using house-made ingredients, fresh juices, and rare liquors in an unpretentious neighborhood setting
attractions.address: 820 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116
attractions.hours: Monday-Sunday: 12:00 PM - 2:00 AM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Famous hand-shaken Ramos Gin Fizz
Extensive menu of classic and innovative cocktails
House-made syrups, tinctures, and freshly squeezed juices
Cozy interior featuring exposed brick and a fireplace
Daily cocktail specials and curated craft beer list
Prime location directly across from Louis Armstrong Park
attractions.tips: The signature Ramos Gin Fizz is a must-try but requires a 15-20 minute wait as it is hand-shaken. Visit during Happy Hour (Mon-Fri, 12-5 PM) or check the daily $5-$8 cocktail specials for great value. It is a popular spot for locals and service industry workers looking to escape the more tourist-heavy areas of the Quarter
attractions.nearby: Louis Armstrong Park, Congo Square, Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, French Market

attractions.name: Bayona
attractions.description: Bayona is a renowned fine-dining destination housed in a historic 1769 Creole cottage in the heart of the French Quarter. Founded by James Beard Award-winning Chef Susan Spicer, the restaurant features a fusion of Mediterranean, Asian, and North African flavors. It is celebrated for its romantic atmosphere, particularly its enchanting outdoor courtyard
attractions.hours: Lunch: Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:30 AM - 2:00 PM; Dinner: Mon - Sat 5:00 PM - 9:00 PM; Sun: Closed ()
attractions.admission: Varies (); 3-course lunch special available for $29 ()
highlights: Housed in a historic 18th-century Creole cottage ()
Award-winning cuisine by Chef Susan Spicer ()
Enchanting outdoor courtyard for al fresco dining ()
Signature dishes include Cream of Garlic Soup and Sweetbreads ()
Extensive and well-curated wine list ()
Romantic interior with warm lighting and fabric-covered walls ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended and can be made up to 60 days in advance; request a table in the courtyard for the best experience on mild evenings (). The fixed-price lunch menu is a popular choice for high-quality dining at a more accessible price point ()
attractions.nearby: Hermann-Grima House, Erin Rose, Bourbon Street, Jackson Square, Historic Voodoo Museum ()

attractions.name: Bourbon Street (Rue Bourbon) ()
attractions.description: Bourbon Street is a historic 13-block stretch in the heart of New Orleans' French Quarter, renowned for its vibrant nightlife and neon-lit atmosphere (). It features a dense concentration of bars, jazz clubs, and legendary restaurants, serving as a primary hub for major celebrations like Mardi Gras and Southern Decadence (). The street's rich history dates back to 1718, and it is famous for its "go-cup" culture, allowing visitors to enjoy beverages while strolling between venues ()
attractions.hours: Open 24/7; individual establishment hours vary, with many bars operating 24 hours a day or until 4:00 AM (,)
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Lafitte’s Blacksmith Shop (one of the oldest buildings used as a bar in the U.S.) ()
Old Absinthe House (historic bar dating back to 1806) ()
Galatoire’s Restaurant (iconic French Creole fine dining established in 1905) ()
Pat O’Brien’s (famous for its courtyard and the invention of the Hurricane cocktail) ()
Musical Legends Park (outdoor venue featuring free live jazz) ()
The "Lavender Line" (the historic center of the LGBTQ+ nightlife scene at St. Ann Street) ()
attractions.tips: Wear closed-toe shoes at night to protect against crowds and spills, and keep an eye on your belongings in large crowds (). Public drinks must be in plastic "go-cups," as glass and cans are prohibited on the street (). For a quieter experience, visit during the morning or early afternoon when the street is more family-friendly ()
attractions.nearby: Royal Street, Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, Preservation Hall, Mississippi Riverfront ()

attractions.name: Brennan's Restaurant
attractions.description: Brennan's is a legendary New Orleans landmark that has defined Creole fine dining since 1946. Located in a historic 18th-century building in the French Quarter, it is world-renowned as the birthplace of Bananas Foster and for its iconic "Breakfast at Brennan's" tradition. The restaurant combines old-world elegance with innovative seasonal offerings and personalized service. ()
attractions.hours: Breakfast/Lunch: Mon-Fri 9:00 AM - 2:00 PM, Sat-Sun 8:00 AM - 2:00 PM; Dinner: Daily 6:00 PM - 10:00 PM; Roost Bar & Courtyard: Mon-Fri 9:00 AM - 10:00 PM, Sat-Sun 8:00 AM - 10:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Varies by menu selection ()
highlights: Birthplace of the world-famous Bananas Foster dessert, flambéed tableside ()
Iconic "Breakfast at Brennan's" tradition featuring Creole-inspired morning classics ()
Friday Courtyard Champagne Sabering ceremony held at 5:00 PM ()
Eight unique dining rooms each with distinct historic decor and old-world elegance ()
Extensive wine cellar and a premier cocktail program at the bird-themed Roost Bar ()
Lush outdoor courtyard providing a classic French Quarter atmosphere for dining and drinks ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended as they book up almost immediately and open 60 days in advance. Adhere to the dress code: no athletic wear, cut-off shorts, or open-toed shoes for gentlemen; jackets are preferred for dinner. ()
attractions.nearby: St. Louis Cathedral, Jackson Square, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, Royal Street Art Galleries, and Napoleon House ()

attractions.name: Café Du Monde
attractions.description: Established in 1862, Café Du Monde is a historic open-air coffee stand located in the French Market of New Orleans. It is world-renowned for its signature café au lait, made with a unique blend of coffee and chicory, and its square French-style beignets generously topped with powdered sugar. As a quintessential New Orleans landmark, it offers a timeless atmosphere near Jackson Square for locals and tourists alike
attractions.hours: Mon-Sun: 7:30 AM - 11:00 PM
attractions.days_open: Open daily; closed on Christmas Day and during major hurricanes
attractions.admission: Free entry; menu prices vary
highlights: Historic coffee stand established in 1862
Signature Café Au Lait
Famous square beignets covered in powdered sugar
Iconic open-air seating with green and white striped awnings
Landmark location in the historic French Market
Traditional experience with views of Jackson Square
attractions.tips: The main French Market location is typically cash-only, so bring plenty of bills; for a faster experience, use the take-out window instead of waiting for a table; visit during off-peak hours (early morning or late night) to avoid the longest lines; beware of the powdered sugar, as it can easily blow onto your clothes
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, French Market, Mississippi Riverfront, Woldenberg Park

attractions.name: Café Maspero
attractions.description: Café Maspero is a landmark French Quarter restaurant known for its classic Louisiana fare and generous portions. Originally established in 1971 at the site of the historic Exchange Coffee House, it now operates from a bustling corner on Decatur Street. The eatery is famous for its massive sandwiches, including the original hot muffuletta and piled-high roast beef po-boys
attractions.address: 601 Decatur St, New Orleans, LA 70130, USA
attractions.hours: Sunday - Wednesday: 8:00 am - 10:00 pm; Thursday: 8:00 am - 11:00 pm; Friday - Saturday: 8:00 am - 12:00 am
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Original Hot Muffuletta sandwich
$1 Strawberry Daiquiris
Bottomless Mimosas and Bloody Marys
Massive Roast Beef Po-Boys and Seafood Platters
Classic Cajun and Creole dishes like Gumbo and Jambalaya
attractions.tips: Arrive early to avoid potential lines, especially during peak lunch hours. Don't miss the $1 frozen strawberry daiquiris for a budget-friendly refreshment. The restaurant is family-friendly and offers a kids' menu
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, Cafe Du Monde, The Shops at JAX Brewery, St. Louis Cathedral

attractions.name: Café du Monde (Original French Market Coffee Stand) ()
attractions.description: Established in 1862, Café du Monde is a world-famous open-air coffee shop located in the heart of the French Market. It is a New Orleans landmark renowned for its iconic beignets—square French-style doughnuts—and chicory-blended café au lait. The cafe offers a traditional, no-frills experience that has remained largely unchanged for over a century (,)
attractions.hours: Sunday-Thursday: 7:15 AM - 11:00 PM; Friday-Saturday: 7:15 AM - 12:00 AM ()
attractions.admission: Free entry; beignets and coffee prices vary (approximately $4-$6 per order) ()
highlights: World-famous beignets served with a thick coating of powdered sugar ()
Traditional Café au Lait made with dark-roasted coffee and chicory ()
Historic open-air setting in the French Market since 1862 ()
Views of Jackson Square and the Mississippi River ()
Take-home beignet mix and coffee tins available for purchase ()
attractions.tips: The original location is cash only, so be sure to have bills ready. Avoid wearing dark clothing as the powdered sugar is famously messy. There is no hostess; look for an empty table, sit down, and a server will come to clean it and take your order (,)
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, French Market, and the Mississippi River waterfront (,)

attractions.name: Cathedral-Basilica of Saint Louis, King of France
attractions.description: St. Louis Cathedral is the oldest continuously active Roman Catholic cathedral in the United States, originally established in 1718. Its iconic triple steeples overlook Jackson Square, serving as a spiritual and architectural centerpiece of the French Quarter. The current structure, completed in 1851, features a unique blend of Greek Revival, Gothic, and Renaissance architectural motifs
attractions.address: 615 Pere Antoine Alley, New Orleans, LA 70116-3291
attractions.hours: Monday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Tuesday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Wednesday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Thursday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Friday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Saturday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM, Sunday: 9:30 AM – 4:00 PM (Last visitors admitted at 3:45 PM; hours subject to change for Mass, weddings, and funerals)
attractions.admission: Free (donations accepted; $1 for self-guided tour brochure; $49 for exclusive guided tour)
highlights: Iconic triple-steeple facade overlooking Jackson Square
Intricate stained glass windows depicting the life of Saint Louis King of France
Beautifully painted ceilings and historic murals
Altar with ornate gold-leaf detailing
St. Anthony’s Garden located behind the cathedral
Statue of Jesus whose shadow projects onto the cathedral at night
attractions.tips: Visit early in the morning to avoid crowds and ensure entry between Mass services. Remove hats before entering and maintain silence as it is an active house of worship. Check the online calendar before visiting, as the cathedral may close for private events like weddings or funerals. Lighting candles is available for a small donation
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, The Cabildo, The Presbytère, Café Du Monde, French Market, Pirate Alley, Mississippi Riverfront

attractions.name: Chartres Street
attractions.description: Chartres Street is a historic and charming thoroughfare in the New Orleans French Quarter, celebrated for its well-preserved architecture and significant landmarks. It serves as a quieter, more dignified alternative to the nearby Bourbon Street, offering a wealth of museums, boutiques, and storied restaurants. The street famously frames Jackson Square and is home to some of the city's oldest structures, reflecting New Orleans' Spanish and French colonial heritage
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (Street access); individual attractions typically 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free to walk; individual museum prices vary (,)
highlights: St. Louis Cathedral: The oldest continuously active cathedral in the United States, overlooking Jackson Square
The Cabildo and The Presbytère: Historic Spanish colonial buildings that now house the Louisiana State Museum
Napoleon House: A landmark restaurant and bar originally intended as a refuge for Napoleon Bonaparte
New Orleans Pharmacy Museum: A beautifully preserved 1823 apothecary showcasing early medical history
Old Ursuline Convent: Completed in 1752, it is the oldest surviving building in the Mississippi River Valley
Jackson Square: The historic heart of the French Quarter, which Chartres Street separates from the cathedral
attractions.tips: To sound like a local, pronounce it as 'CHART-ers.' It is one of the most walkable and photogenic streets in the French Quarter, offering a peaceful atmosphere compared to Bourbon Street. Be sure to try a Pimm’s Cup at Napoleon House and explore the unique legends of the 'Casket Girls' at the Old Ursuline Convent
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, Royal Street, French Market, Mississippi River, Bourbon Street

attractions.name: Erin Rose
attractions.description: Erin Rose is a quintessential neighborhood bar located just steps from Bourbon Street in the heart of the French Quarter. Known for its unpretentious dive-bar atmosphere, it serves as a casual meeting place where locals, artists, and tourists mingle. The bar is famously home to the Frozen Irish Coffee and the "internationally inspired" Killer Poboys
attractions.hours: Daily: 10:00 AM – 7:00 AM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Famous Frozen Irish Coffee made with a secret recipe
Home to Killer Poboys, offering chef-crafted sandwiches at the back bar
"Wake Up and Live!" daily drink specials available from 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM
Historic French Quarter location in a former 19th-century single-family home
Late-night service industry specials on Monday through Wednesday
Relaxed, eclectic atmosphere with a jukebox and local vibe
attractions.tips: Visit between 10:00 AM and 2:00 PM for the "Wake Up and Live!" specials to get the best prices on their signature Frozen Irish Coffee and Bloody Marys. Be aware that the Killer Poboys kitchen in the back is closed on Tuesdays
attractions.nearby: Bourbon Street, Mambo's, Oceana Grill, Deanie's Seafood

attractions.name: French Quarter (Vieux Carré Historic District) ()
attractions.description: The French Quarter, also known as the Vieux Carré, is the oldest and most famous neighborhood in New Orleans, characterized by its historic Spanish and French colonial architecture and iconic cast-iron balconies (). It serves as the city's cultural heart, blending 18th-century charm with a vibrant atmosphere of jazz clubs, street performers, and world-class dining (). Visitors can explore 78 square blocks filled with museums, art galleries, and historic landmarks such as Jackson Square and the French Market ()
attractions.address: Bounded by the Mississippi River, North Rampart Street, Canal Street, and Esplanade Avenue ()
attractions.hours: Neighborhood: 24/7; Visitor Center: Tue-Sat 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Closed Sun-Mon ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Jackson Square and the St. Louis Cathedral ()
Bourbon Street’s legendary nightlife and jazz clubs ()
Royal Street’s world-renowned antique shops and art galleries ()
The historic French Market, operating for over 200 years ()
Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop, one of the oldest operating bars in the U.S. ()
Preservation Hall, a traditional jazz institution ()
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes for the cobblestone streets and stay aware of your surroundings, especially after dark (). Don't miss a stop at Cafe Du Monde for iconic beignets and cafe au lait, but be prepared for a line (). Visit during the day to appreciate the architecture and at night for live music ()
attractions.nearby: Canal Street, Woldenberg Park, Faubourg Marigny (Frenchmen Street), and Louis Armstrong Park in the Tremé ()

attractions.name: Fritzel's European Jazz Pub
attractions.description: Fritzel's European Jazz Pub is situated in a historic 1831 building in the heart of the French Quarter and is known as the "Home of Traditional New Orleans Jazz." Since its founding in 1969, it has become a mecca for jazz lovers and musicians alike, offering live music nightly in an intimate atmosphere. The club is celebrated as the oldest traditional jazz club still operating on Bourbon Street
attractions.hours: Sunday – Thursday: 10:00 AM – Late; Friday – Saturday: 10:00 AM – Late
attractions.admission: No cover charge; one drink minimum per person per set
highlights: Located in a historic building dating back to 1831
Oldest traditional jazz club operating on Bourbon Street, founded in 1969
Nightly live traditional New Orleans jazz performances
Intimate and authentic atmosphere described as a "home away from home" for jazz aficionados
Features the Fritzel's Jazz Band and other talented local musicians
Open 365 days a year
attractions.tips: There is a one-drink minimum requirement per person for each jazz set. The venue is strictly 21+ only. It is a small and cozy space, so arrive early before a set starts to secure a seat, and be prepared for a rotating lineup of talented musicians. While there is no cover charge, tipping the band is highly encouraged and customary
attractions.nearby: Bourbon Street, Preservation Hall, Jackson Square, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, Frenchmen Street

attractions.name: GW Fins
attractions.description: GW Fins is an upscale seafood destination in the heart of the French Quarter that prides itself on serving the freshest seasonal seafood from around the world. The restaurant features a menu that changes daily to highlight the day's best catch, prepared with techniques that emphasize natural flavors and elegant simplicity
attractions.hours: Monday - Thursday: 5:00 PM - 9:30 PM; Friday - Saturday: 5:00 PM - 10:00 PM; Sunday: 5:00 PM - 9:30 PM
attractions.admission: Varies; fine dining dinner menu
highlights: Daily printed menu featuring the freshest global and local seafood catches
Signature 'Scalibut' dish featuring halibut and sea scallops
Innovative 'ocean conservation cuts' and whole-fish butchery on refrigerated tables
Extensive wine program with over 100 varieties and 70 available by the glass
Acclaimed fine dining experience recognized as a James Beard Award semifinalist
Famous warm, buttery biscuits served to every guest upon arrival
attractions.tips: Reservations are strongly encouraged as the restaurant is highly popular. Guests should adhere to the dress code, which requires collared shirts for gentlemen and prohibits athletic wear. Complimentary validated parking is available at the Standard Parking Garage on Iberville and Dauphine
attractions.nearby: Germaine Wells Mardi Gras Museum, Larry Flynt's Hustler Club, Old Absinthe House, Bourbon Street

attractions.name: Galatoire's Restaurant
attractions.description: Founded in 1905, Galatoire's is a historic cornerstone of New Orleans fine dining, renowned for its French-Creole cuisine and preservation of local culinary traditions. Located on iconic Bourbon Street, the restaurant is currently managed by the fifth generation of the Galatoire family and descendants. It remains a legendary destination for its festive atmosphere, particularly during its famous Friday lunches
attractions.hours: Monday: Closed; Tuesday – Saturday: 11:30 a.m. – 9 p.m.; Sunday: 12 p.m. – 9 p.m
attractions.admission: Varies by menu selection; appetizers from ~$11, entrees ~$30-$65
highlights: Authentic French-Creole cuisine and seafood
Historic 1905 establishment on Bourbon Street
Legendary "Friday Lunch" social tradition
Elegant dining environment with a strict dress code
Recipient of numerous culinary accolades, including James Beard Awards
Multi-generational family-owned and operated legacy
attractions.tips: Men are required to wear collared shirts and long pants; jackets are mandatory after 5 p.m. and all day Sunday. Friday lunch is a high-energy local tradition and can be quite loud; reservations are highly recommended for the main dining room. For parties of six or more, call the restaurant directly for reservations
attractions.nearby: Hotel Monteleone, GW Fins, Deanie's Seafood, Bourbon Street, Olde Nola Cookery

attractions.name: Gallier House
attractions.description: Gallier House is a meticulously restored Victorian French Quarter townhouse completed in 1860 by the prominent architect James Gallier, Jr. as his private family residence. The museum showcases innovative 19th-century engineering, including early indoor plumbing and an experimental skylight, while offering a comprehensive look at the lives of both the Gallier family and the enslaved people who worked on the property. Tours provide insight into the social history, decorative arts, and architectural advancements of New Orleans during the Victorian era
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Tuesday: Closed; Wednesday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Thursday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Friday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Saturday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Sunday: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM (Tours at 10:30 AM, 11:30 AM, 1:30 PM, 2:30 PM, and 3:30 PM)
attractions.days_open: Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday, Monday
attractions.admission: Adults: $20.00; Military, Seniors (65+), Students (18+), and Children (8-18): $17.00; Children under 8: Free. Combination tickets for both Gallier House and Hermann-Grima House are available for $34.00 (Adults) or $29.00 (Discounted tiers)
highlights: Architectural design by famed New Orleans architect James Gallier, Jr
Innovative 19th-century indoor plumbing featuring hot and cold running water
Original experimental skylight and ornate Victorian interior décor
Intact attached slave quarters and courtyard
Iconic "Paris Green" cast iron gate designed specifically for the residence
Rotating seasonal exhibitions including Creole Death & Mourning and Christmas displays
attractions.tips: Reservations for guided tours are highly encouraged as the museum is accessible by guided tour only. Consider purchasing a combination ticket if you plan to visit the sister site, Hermann-Grima House, for a significant discount. Photography is permitted without flash or tripods, but large bags and umbrellas must be checked at the museum shop
attractions.nearby: Hermann-Grima House, Beauregard-Keyes House, Old Ursuline Convent Museum, Lalaurie Mansion, French Market

Led by Master Storytellers, the walking tour takes guests through the French Quarter to explore sinister spirits, mysteries, and the real story of the LaLaurie Mansion. ()
attractions.address: 718 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116
attractions.hours: 6:00 PM and 8:00 PM nightly ()

highlights: Led by Master Storytellers fully licensed by the City of New Orleans ()
Historically accurate tales based on researched books, archives, and records ()

attractions.tips: Book your tickets online in advance to save $3 per person. Arrive at least 30 minutes to an hour early to pick up your tickets at the Voodoo Lounge and enjoy the BOGO drink special. Wear comfortable walking shoes as the tour covers approximately one mile. Cameras are encouraged for capturing potential paranormal activity, but video recording is generally prohibited without prior permission. (), ()

attractions.name: Goldring Woldenberg Riverfront Park
attractions.description: Goldring Woldenberg Riverfront Park is a 16-acre sprawling green space located between the Mississippi River and the historic French Quarter. Created in the late 1980s on the site of former industrial warehouses, it offers picturesque views of the river and serves as a tranquil escape from the city's bustle. The park features lush landscaping, bricked walkways, and numerous significant public art installations.,)
attractions.hours: Sunday – Thursday: 6 AM – 10 PM; Friday & Saturday: 6 AM – Midnight
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Audubon Aquarium and Insectarium located at the upriver end
90-foot linear water feature (splash pad) in front of the Aquarium
Significant public art including the New Orleans Holocaust Memorial and Monument to the Immigrant
The Moon Walk promenade connecting the park to Jackson Square
Panoramic views of the Mississippi River and the Crescent City Connection bridge
The 'NOLA Front Porch' open gathering area and marsh-themed playground
attractions.tips: The park does not have public restrooms; visitors should use facilities inside the Aquarium or nearby businesses. It is an excellent vantage point for watching steamboats and river traffic. Dogs are welcome but must remain leashed at all times. Check the community calendar for frequent festivals and live music events like the French Quarter Festival
attractions.nearby: Audubon Aquarium, Audubon Insectarium, Jackson Square, The Shops at JAX Brewery, Café Du Monde, French Market, Spanish Plaza, Steamboat Natchez

attractions.name: Haunted History Tours
attractions.description: Haunted History Tours is New Orleans' original ghost tour company, providing historically accurate and entertaining explorations of the city's supernatural past since 1995. Their expert guides lead visitors through the French Quarter and Garden District, sharing documented tales of ghosts, voodoo, vampires, and unsolved mysteries. The tours are designed to be "mildly theatrical and hugely historical," offering a deep dive into the legends that have made New Orleans famous
attractions.address: 723 St Peter, New Orleans, LA 70116, USA
attractions.hours: Walking tours are typically offered at 5:00 PM and 8:00 PM nightly; Cemetery tours at 10:00 AM and 1:15 PM; Bus tours at 7:00 PM and 9:30 PM; specific tour times vary by season and day
attractions.admission: Walking tours start from $30 for adults, $25 for seniors/students/military, and $20 for children (ages 6-12); bus tours start from $55 for adults and $45 for seniors/students/military; children 5 and under are free
highlights: Historically accurate stories based on extensive research and police archives
Visits to infamous landmarks like the LaLaurie Mansion and St. Louis Cemetery
Featured on major networks including A&E, History Channel, and Travel Channel
Variety of tour types: walking tours, bus tours, cemetery visits, and haunted pub crawls
Licensed 5-star local guides known for engaging and non-scripted storytelling
attractions.tips: Advance reservations are highly recommended, especially during the peak October season and on weekends. Tours operate rain or shine, so dress for the weather and wear comfortable walking shoes. Arrive at least 30 minutes prior to departure to check in and secure your spot
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, Preservation Hall, Cafe Du Monde, Bourbon Street

attractions.name: Hermann-Grima Historic House
attractions.description: Built in 1831, the Hermann-Grima House is a meticulously restored Federal-style mansion and National Historic Landmark in the French Quarter. The museum offers a window into 19th-century New Orleans life, showcasing the history of its owners and the enslaved people who lived and worked on the property. It features unique architectural elements, including the only extant horse stable and a functional 1830s open-hearth kitchen in the district
attractions.address: 820 St. Louis Street, New Orleans, LA 70112
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Tuesday: Closed, Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Sunday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adults: $20.00, Military/Seniors (65+)/Students (18+)/Children (8-18): $17.00, Children Under 8: Free
highlights: Federal-style architecture and Creole sensibilities
Original 1830s open-hearth kitchen with seasonal cooking demonstrations
The only remaining original and intact horse stable in the French Quarter
Preserved urban slave quarters highlighting the history of enslavement
Lush courtyard garden and meticulously restored 19th-century interiors
Extensive collection of original family artifacts and period pieces
attractions.tips: Guided tours are led by docents and last approximately one hour; reservations are highly encouraged. Large bags and umbrellas must be checked at the shop, and photography is permitted without flashes or tripods. Note that the museum is closed on Tuesdays and major holidays, including the days leading up to Mardi Gras
attractions.nearby: Gallier House, Bourbon Street, St. Louis Cemetery No. 1, Jackson Square, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, The Historic New Orleans Collection

attractions.name: Historic BK House & Gardens (also known as Beauregard-Keyes House)
attractions.description: The Historic BK House & Gardens is a National Historic Landmark in the French Quarter, originally built in 1826 as a raised center-hall villa. It blends Creole and American architectural styles and is named for two of its most famous residents: Confederate General P.G.T. Beauregard and bestselling author Frances Parkinson Keyes. Today, it serves as a museum and cultural site that preserves the history of the diverse families and individuals who lived and labored on the property over two centuries
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM (Tours on the hour at 10, 11, and 1–3 PM); Tuesday: 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM; Wednesday: CLOSED; Thursday: 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM; Friday: 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM; Saturday: 10:00 AM – 3:00 PM; Sunday: CLOSED
attractions.admission: Adults: $15.00; Senior Citizens: $12.50; Students: $12.50; Military: $10.00; Children (ages 6–12): $7.50; Children (under 6): Free
highlights: Guided tours of the restored main house and period parterre garden
Frances Parkinson Keyes' 1950s writing studio and her extensive collection of over 200 antique dolls and 87 teapots
Unique architectural fusion of Federal-style front facade and raised Creole cottage form
Preserved dependency buildings, including original slave quarters and a kitchen
Association with world chess champion Paul Morphy, who was born in the house
Beautiful formal garden with a cast-iron fountain, often used for weddings and special events
attractions.tips: Tours begin on the hour, so visitors should arrive slightly before the start time to secure a spot. Reservations are not required for individual tours but are accepted; however, group tours of 25 or more must be scheduled in advance. The house is noted for its ghost stories and is a popular stop on local paranormal tours
attractions.nearby: Old Ursuline Convent Museum (directly across the street), French Market, St. Louis Cathedral, Jackson Square, and the New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park

attractions.name: Jackson Square ()
attractions.description: Jackson Square is a historic 2.5-acre park and National Historic Landmark located in the heart of the French Quarter (). Originally known as the Place d'Armes, it is famous for its open-air artist colony and as the site where the Louisiana Purchase was ceremonially signed in 1803 ()). The square is anchored by the St. Louis Cathedral and surrounded by historic museums and the Mississippi River ()
attractions.hours: Daily 8:00 AM – 6:00 PM (Winter); Daily 8:00 AM – 7:00 PM (Daylight Savings Time) ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Equestrian statue of General Andrew Jackson ())
St. Louis Cathedral, the oldest cathedral in North America ()
The Cabildo and The Presbytère museums ()
Vibrant open-air artist colony and street performers ()
Historic Pontalba Buildings, the oldest apartments in the U.S. ()
Moon Walk riverfront promenade overlooking the Mississippi ())
attractions.tips: Visit early in the morning to avoid crowds and enjoy the quiet beauty of the square (). Bring cash to tip the talented street performers and local artists that line the fence (). Be sure to try a beignet at the world-famous Café Du Monde located just across the street ()
attractions.nearby: St. Louis Cathedral, Café Du Monde, The Cabildo, The Presbytère, French Market, Steamboat Natchez ()

attractions.name: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve - French Quarter Visitor Center
attractions.description: The Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve's French Quarter Visitor Center serves as an educational hub exploring the cultural and natural history of the Mississippi River Delta. Housed in a historic 19th-century former candy factory, the center features interactive exhibits, a short film, and daily ranger-led programs. It offers a unique look at the diverse influences that shaped New Orleans and the surrounding region. ()
attractions.hours: Tuesday: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Wednesday: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Thursday: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Friday: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Saturday: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Sunday: Closed, Monday: Closed ()

"What's Cooking Wednesday" food demonstrations ()
Junior Ranger program for kids ()
attractions.tips: Enter through the open-air alleyway to view informational signs before reaching the courtyard. This site is known for having some of the few public restrooms in the French Quarter. Arrive early for the 10:00 AM ranger talk to get oriented to the area. (,)
attractions.nearby: Jackson Square, Mississippi River, New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park (located within), Canal Street Streetcar Line ()

attractions.name: Jean Lafitte's Old Absinthe House
attractions.description: The Old Absinthe House is a legendary historic bar and landmark located in the heart of the French Quarter. Built in 1806, it is world-renowned as the birthplace of the Absinthe Frappé and has hosted famous figures ranging from Jean Lafitte to Oscar Wilde and Mark Twain. The tavern maintains an old-world atmosphere with its iconic copper-topped bar and walls covered in business cards from past patrons (,)
attractions.hours: Monday: 9 am - 2 am; Tuesday: 9 am - 2 am; Wednesday: 9 am - 2 am; Thursday: 9 am - 3 am; Friday: 9 am - 4 am; Saturday: 9 am - 4 am; Sunday: 9 am - 3 am ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Historic 1806 building originally used as a commission house and grocery ()
Birthplace of the famous Absinthe Frappé, created by Cayetano Ferrer in 1874 ()
Iconic copper-topped wooden bar that has served patrons for nearly two centuries ()
Legendary meeting site for General Andrew Jackson and pirate Jean Lafitte to plan the Battle of New Orleans ()
Extensive selection of traditional absinthe drips and specialty cocktails ()
attractions.tips: Be sure to try the signature Absinthe Frappé; look for the thousands of business cards and historical artifacts pinned to the walls; visit during the day for a quieter look at the history or at night for the full Bourbon Street experience ()
attractions.nearby: Bourbon Street, Arnaud's Restaurant, Jackson Square, St. Louis Cathedral, Preservation Hall ()

attractions.name: Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop Bar ()
attractions.description: Built between 1722 and 1732, Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop is reputed to be the oldest structure used as a bar in the United States (). Located at the corner of Bourbon and St. Philip Streets, it was reportedly used by the Lafitte brothers, Jean and Pierre, as a base for their smuggling operations (). The building survived major fires in the late 18th century due to its slate roofing and remains a rare example of French Provincial Louis XV construction ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Reputed oldest structure used as a bar in the United States ()
Famous for its signature Purple Voodoo Daiquiri (Purple Drank) ()
Atmospheric candlelight-only lighting in the evenings ()
Rare extant example of briquette-entre-poteaux (brick-between-post) construction ()
National Historic Landmark with a history tied to privateer Jean Lafitte ()
Live piano bar music sessions ()
attractions.tips: The bar is primarily cash only, so be prepared, though some recent reports suggest credit cards might be accepted (). It is known to be quite dark and atmospheric at night as it relies heavily on candlelight (). Try the famous Purple Voodoo Daiquiri, but be aware it is quite strong (). Visit during the day to appreciate the historic architecture or at night for the haunted, candlelit vibe ()
attractions.nearby: Lafitte-In-Exile, Bourbon Street, St. Philip Street, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, LaLaurie Mansion (,)

attractions.name: Le Petit Théâtre du Vieux Carré
attractions.description: Le Petit Théâtre du Vieux Carré is New Orleans' most historic playhouse, established in 1916 as a cornerstone of the American little theatre movement. Located in the heart of the French Quarter since 1922, it presents a diverse annual season of professional-quality plays and musicals. The theatre serves the community through high-quality performances and extensive educational programs, including a Young Conservatory for aspiring artists ()
attractions.address: 616 St. Peter Street, New Orleans, LA 70116

attractions.name: Maison Bourbon Jazz Club
attractions.description: Maison Bourbon Jazz Club is one of Bourbon Street's oldest and most iconic live jazz venues, dedicated to the preservation of traditional New Orleans jazz. This legendary spot has served as an apprenticeship ground for many notable musicians, including Harry Connick Jr., and remains one of only two dedicated jazz clubs on Bourbon Street. Visitors can experience authentic Dixieland and traditional jazz in a historic setting that captures the essence of the city's musical heritage (,)
attractions.hours: Monday - Thursday: 11:00 AM - 1:00 AM; Friday - Saturday: 11:00 AM - 2:00 AM; Sunday: 11:00 AM - 1:00 AM. Live shows typically start hourly at half-past the hour beginning at 7:30 PM (Mon-Thu, Sun), 3:30 PM (Fri-Sat Day), and 8:30 PM (Fri-Sat Night) (,)
attractions.admission: No cover charge; one drink minimum per set required ()
highlights: Dedicated to the preservation of traditional and Dixieland jazz ()
One of the oldest live jazz clubs on Bourbon Street ()
Expansive balcony overlooking the heart of the French Quarter ()
Live jazz performances every night of the week ()
Features a quaint courtyard connecting to My Bar @ 635 ()
Historical apprenticeship site for world-renowned musicians like Harry Connick Jr. ()
attractions.tips: There is a one-drink minimum per set (sets last about 45 minutes), and it is customary to tip the band. The venue is mostly wheelchair accessible on the main level and through the courtyard, though restroom access may be narrow. It is an ideal spot for those seeking an authentic alternative to the more modern pop-focused bars on Bourbon Street (,)
attractions.nearby: My Bar @ 635, Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop, Jackson Square, New Orleans Pharmacy Museum, Bourbon Street (,)

attractions.name: Marie Laveau's House of Voodoo
attractions.description: Marie Laveau’s House of Voodoo is a historic shop and museum located in the New Orleans French Quarter, situated on the former residence of the legendary Voodoo Queen Marie Laveau. Established in 1978, the shop offers an authentic selection of voodoo dolls, talismans, and spiritual items alongside a public altar and professional psychic readings. It serves as a mystical gateway for visitors to explore the rich history and traditions of Louisiana Voodoo in a respectful and serene environment (,)
attractions.hours: Sunday – Thursday: 10:00 AM – 11:00 PM; Friday – Saturday: 10:00 AM – 12:00 AM ()
attractions.admission: Free to browse the shop (); approximately $8 for the museum area (); psychic readings are approximately $50 for 20 minutes ()
highlights: Handmade voodoo dolls, gris-gris bags, and unique talismans ()
Daily on-site psychic and spiritual readings ()
Historic location on the site of Marie Laveau's original home ()
Collection of tribal masks and statues from around the world ()
Voodoo Altar for offerings and spiritual items ()
attractions.tips: Photography is strictly prohibited inside the shop to respect the spiritual items and patrons (). To secure a spot for psychic readings, it is recommended to arrive early in the day as appointments are made on a same-day basis (). Be sure to look up at the ceiling to see a large hidden statue that many visitors overlook (). Visiting during weekday mornings is the best way to avoid the larger Bourbon Street crowds ()
attractions.nearby: Preservation Hall, Jackson Square, Napoleon House, St. Louis Cathedral, and Reverend Zombie’s Voodoo Shop (,)

attractions.name: Musée Conti Wax Museum ()
attractions.description: The Musée Conti Wax Museum was a long-standing attraction in the French Quarter that depicted over 300 years of New Orleans history through more than 147 life-size wax figures (). Opened in 1964, it showcased pivotal historical moments, cultural legends like Marie Laveau, and featured a popular "Haunted Dungeon" section dedicated to classic horror literature and films (). The museum officially closed its doors in January 2016, with a portion of its collection later relocated to the Great River Road Museum at Houmas House ()
attractions.hours: Historically: Monday – Saturday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM; Sunday: 12:00 PM – 5:00 PM (). Also reported as open daily 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (). Currently: Permanently Closed
attractions.days_open: Historically open Daily () or Monday through Saturday (). Currently: Permanently Closed
attractions.admission: Historically: Adults $19.95, Seniors $15.95, Children (under 12) $12.95 (). Previously listed as Adults $8 () and $7 (). Currently: Permanently Closed
highlights: Over 147 life-size wax figures depicting Louisiana history ()
The Haunted Dungeon featuring horror icons like Frankenstein, Dracula, and the Phantom of the Opera ()
Historically accurate dioramas of the Battle of New Orleans and Marie Laveau’s Voodoo ceremonies ()
A unique diorama of Napoleon Bonaparte in a bathtub meditating on the Louisiana Purchase ()
Vivid historical tableaus including the 1850s Storyville 'cat-fight' scene and Madame LaLaurie's mansion ()
Wax figures crafted in Paris with real human hair and glass eyes ()
attractions.tips: While now closed, it was known as a 'hidden gem' and 'New Orleans' best kept secret' for a creepy but highly educational introduction to city history (). It was particularly recommended for teens and fans of historical kitsch, though some displays were considered too macabre for young children (). Visitors who still wish to see the figures can find about 50 of them on display at the Great River Road Museum in Darrow, LA ()

attractions.name: NOLA Restaurant
attractions.description: NOLA was chef/restaurateur Emeril Lagasse’s hip and casual eatery located in a renovated warehouse in the heart of the French Quarter. Opened in 1992, it fused traditional Creole, Acadian, and Southern cuisine with global influences and local ingredients. The restaurant featured a three-story layout with an open-action kitchen and a signature wood-fired brick oven

attractions.name: New Orleans Culinary History Tours
attractions.description: New Orleans Culinary History Tours (also known as The Premier New Orleans Food Tour) offers an immersive walking experience through the French Quarter, blending the city's rich history with its iconic flavors. Led by passionate local guides, the small-group tours visit four historic restaurants to sample quintessential dishes like jambalaya, gumbo, and crawfish etouffee. Participants learn about the diverse cultural influences—including French, Spanish, African, and Sicilian—that shaped New Orleans' world-famous culinary landscape
attractions.hours: 1:00 PM – 4:00 PM on tour days
attractions.days_open: Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays on select dates

highlights: Small-group walking tours led by expert local guides
Visits to four historic and upscale French Quarter restaurants

Educational storytelling on the history of Cajun and Creole traditions
Final stop for a classic Creole pecan praline dessert
attractions.tips: Advanced booking is required as groups are kept small; arrive at the Market Cafe meeting point 15 minutes early. The tour operates rain or shine, so comfortable walking shoes and weather-appropriate clothing are recommended. While ice water is served at stops, adult beverages and soft drinks are available for purchase separately

attractions.name: New Orleans French Market
attractions.description: The French Market is a historic open-air market spanning six blocks in New Orleans, dating back to 1791. It features a diverse collection of local artisans, fresh produce, and unique retail shops, serving as a symbol of pride and progress for the city. Visitors can enjoy iconic New Orleans food, live music, and a vibrant community atmosphere while exploring the market's historic architecture

attractions.description: Established in 1972, this historic institution preserves and shares the often misunderstood culture and folklore of Louisiana Voodoo. Located in the heart of the French Quarter, the museum features an intimate collection of authentic artifacts, ritual altars, and exhibits dedicated to legendary figures like Voodoo Queen Marie Laveau. It serves as a cultural bridge, educating visitors on the religion's deep roots in West African traditions and its unique evolution in New Orleans

attractions.name: New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park
attractions.description: New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park was established in 1994 to celebrate the origins, early history, development, and progression of jazz, which is recognized as a rare and valuable national treasure. The park provides visitors with live performances, educational programs, and interpretative sites that preserve the cultural legacy of America's unique musical art form. It is a 'living' park that both shares place-based history and actively participates in the city's vibrant music scene through its performing park rangers
attractions.hours: Tuesday: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, Wednesday: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, Thursday: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, Friday: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, Saturday: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM, Sunday: Closed, Monday: Closed
attractions.admission: Free (No entrance pass or fee required for any of the park's programs or visitor centers)
highlights: Live jazz performances and concerts hosted by the Arrowhead Jazz Band
Ranger-led music demonstrations and educational talks using various instruments
Congo Square in Louis Armstrong Park, the historic gathering place for enslaved Africans
Self-guided Jazz walking tours with audio clips accessible via mobile app or phone
Interactive exhibits and Junior Ranger programs for children at the visitor center
attractions.tips: Check the official park calendar online before visiting as many live performances and ranger talks occur at different times and locations, including the New Orleans Jazz Museum. Arrive early for ranger-led walking tours, as they often have a limited capacity and may require sign-up. Most park rangers are professional musicians themselves, so don't miss the chance to hear them perform and share their unique insights

attractions.name: New Orleans Pharmacy Museum
attractions.description: Housed in the 1823 Creole townhouse of Louis J. Dufilho Jr., the first licensed pharmacist in the United States, this museum showcases the extensive history of pharmacy and healthcare. The collection includes over 10,000 artifacts ranging from antique apothecary bottles and surgical tools to voodoo potions and a mid-19th century soda fountain. Visitors can explore two floors of exhibits that provide a window into the evolution of medicine and the unique cultural history of New Orleans
attractions.address: 514 Chartres St, New Orleans, LA 70130, USA

attractions.name: New Orleans School of Cooking
attractions.description: Established in 1980, the New Orleans School of Cooking offers entertaining classes focused on the "fun, food, and folklore" of Louisiana cuisine. Located in a renovated 1830s molasses warehouse, the school features expert chefs who teach Cajun and Creole specialties like gumbo, jambalaya, and pralines. Guests can choose from demonstration-style lessons, interactive hands-on sessions, or specialty mixology and lab classes

Spanning over 7,000 square feet in the French Quarter, the museum features historical artifacts, theatrical sets, and multimedia recreations that trace the city's past from its 1718 founding to the closure of the district in 1917 (). Visitors can explore reconstructed saloons and brothel parlors while learning about the birth of jazz and the colorful figures who once inhabited "The District" ()

It is a premier example of French Colonial public architecture and currently serves as a museum and part of the Catholic Cultural Heritage Center. The site includes the historic convent building, St. Mary's Church, and beautiful formal gardens. ()

The venue features multiple areas including a lively main bar, a world-renowned dueling piano lounge, and a spacious 4,000-square-foot outdoor patio with its landmark Flaming Fountain. It stands as one of the nation's finest examples of formal Spanish colonial public architecture

It serves as a place of worship and education, offering services to people of all races and nationalities to foster enlightenment and dispel common misconceptions about Voodoo. The center includes an altar room, an herb apothecary, and a gift shop filled with handcrafted spiritual items

Street parking is the only option available, so plan accordingly during busy events or holidays. attractions.nearby: Superior Seafood & Oyster Bar, Shaya, La Petite Grocery, Magazine Street, Audubon Park, Loyola University New Orleans, Tulane University

attractions.name: Commander's Palace
attractions.description: Commander's Palace is a landmark Louisiana Creole restaurant known for its innovative 'Haute Creole' cuisine and legendary jazz brunch. Established in 1893, it has been operated by the Brennan family since 1974 and is a cornerstone of New Orleans' culinary heritage. The restaurant is famous for its turquoise-and-white Victorian architecture and its history of nurturing world-class chefs. (,)
attractions.hours: Lunch: Wednesday–Friday 11:30 AM; Dinner: Monday–Tuesday 5:30 PM, Wednesday–Sunday 6:00 PM; Jazz Brunch: Saturday 11:00 AM–2:00 PM, Sunday 10:00 AM–2:00 PM ()
attractions.days_open: Monday–Sunday (Closed Mardi Gras Day and Christmas Day) ()
attractions.admission: Prices vary; typical lunch entrées $23–$44, lunch special $49, Eat Fit menu $46 ()
highlights: Innovative Haute Creole cuisine by Executive Chef Meg Bickford
Legendary Jazz Brunch on Saturdays and Sundays with live music
Famous 25¢ Martinis available during weekday lunch with an entrée purchase
Historic Victorian building with iconic turquoise-and-white striped awnings
Award-winning wine program and professional tableside service features like 'Turtle Soup au Sherry'
Nurtured culinary legends including Emeril Lagasse and Paul Prudhomme
attractions.tips: Strict dress code: Business attire is required; jackets are preferred for gentlemen, while collared shirts and closed-toed shoes are mandatory. No shorts, t-shirts, flip-flops, or hats for men are allowed, and jeans are discouraged. Reservations are highly recommended and often necessary weeks in advance, especially for the popular Jazz Brunch. ()

attractions.name: Garden District
attractions.description: The Garden District is a historic neighborhood in New Orleans famous for its remarkably preserved collection of 19th-century mansions and lush, oak-lined streets. Originally developed between 1832 and 1900, it served as an opulent residential enclave for wealthy newcomers who preferred a more spacious, suburban alternative to the French Quarter. Today, it stands as one of the most architecturally significant districts in the Southern United States, characterized by grand Greek Revival and Victorian homes
attractions.address: Bounded by St. Charles Avenue (North), 1st Street (East), Magazine Street (South), and Toledano Street (West)
attractions.hours: Neighborhood: Open 24 hours daily; Lafayette Cemetery No. 1: Generally 7:00 AM – 3:00 PM (Note: Access may be restricted for maintenance)
attractions.admission: Free to explore the neighborhood; specific attractions and guided tours vary in price
highlights: St. Charles Avenue Streetcar ride
Historic mansions such as the Buckner Mansion and Bradish Johnson House
Commander’s Palace legendary Creole restaurant
Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 historic above-ground tombs
Magazine Street boutique shopping and dining
Picturesque live oak canopies and ornate ironwork architecture
attractions.tips: The most scenic way to arrive is via the St. Charles Streetcar. Wear comfortable walking shoes as the sidewalks are often uneven due to the roots of the ancient live oaks. Please be respectful of the residents and private property while photographing the historic homes
attractions.nearby: Audubon Zoo & Park, Irish Channel, Lower Garden District, National WWII Museum

attractions.name: Lafayette Cemetery No. 1
attractions.description: Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 is a historic, non-segregated municipal cemetery located in the heart of New Orleans' Garden District. Established in 1833, it is renowned for its above-ground tombs, elaborate society vaults, and unique cruciform layout designed to accommodate funeral processions. As a National Historic Landmark, it serves as a significant example of the city's unique burial traditions and "Cities of the Dead" (,)
attractions.hours: Closed to the public for repairs (Historical hours: Mon–Fri 7:00 AM – 3:00 PM, Sat 8:00 AM – 12:00 PM) (,)
attractions.admission: Free (Historically); Currently closed to the public (,)
highlights: First planned cemetery in New Orleans with a unique cruciform layout ()
Massive society tombs including the Jefferson Fire Company No. 22 and the Society for the Relief of Destitute Orphan Boys ()
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places and designated a National Historic Landmark ()
Famous filming location for movies like 'Interview with the Vampire' and TV shows like 'The Originals' ()
Contains approximately 1,100 family tombs and over 7,000 interred individuals ()
attractions.tips: While the cemetery is currently closed for repairs, you can still view many of its most famous tombs through the iron gates along Washington Avenue. It is best visited as part of a larger Garden District walking tour, and many visitors enjoy stopping at Commander's Palace directly across the street for a meal or drink (,)
attractions.nearby: Commander's Palace restaurant, Garden District Book Shop, St. Charles Avenue Streetcar, and Buckner Mansion (,)

attractions.name: St. Charles Streetcar Line
attractions.description: The St. Charles Streetcar Line is the oldest continuously operating streetcar line in the world, having been in service since 1835. It is a National Historic Landmark that carries passengers in vintage dark green cars along a scenic 6-mile route through some of the city's most historic neighborhoods. The line is famous for its leisurely pace and views of grand antebellum mansions and ancient live oak trees

Designated as a National Historic Landmark
Iconic vintage Perley Thomas streetcars from the 1920s

attractions.name: Backstreet Cultural Museum
attractions.description: The Backstreet Cultural Museum preserves and perpetuates the unique cultural traditions of New Orleans' African American society through its collections of costumes, artifacts, and photographs. A pillar of the Tremé community, the museum showcases Mardi Gras Indians, jazz funerals, social aid and pleasure clubs, and other processional traditions

Memorabilia and films from over 30 years of jazz funerals
Costumes and objects from the North Side Skull and Bone Gang
Exhibits detailing the Baby Dolls carnival tradition
A comprehensive film archive documenting over 500 cultural events

attractions.name: Congo Square
attractions.description: Congo Square is a historic open space located in the Tremé neighborhood of New Orleans, famously known as the birthplace of jazz music. During the 18th and 19th centuries, it served as a unique gathering place where enslaved Africans and free people of color could meet on Sundays to trade, dance, and play music. This cultural exchange preserved African traditions and laid the foundation for quintessential American musical forms like jazz and rhythm and blues
attractions.address: 701 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116-3085
attractions.hours: Daily 8:00 AM – 6:00 PM (Winter); 8:00 AM – 7:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Birthplace of Jazz: Historically significant as the primary site where African musical traditions were preserved and evolved into jazz
Sunday Drum Circles: Continuing tradition of communal drumming and dancing every Sunday afternoon
Louis Armstrong Park: Located within a 31-acre park dedicated to New Orleans' most famous musical son
Statues and Monuments: Features a bronze sculpture of jazz pioneer Buddy Bolden and a memorial to Louis Armstrong
National Register of Historic Places: Officially recognized as a National Historic Landmark since 1993
Cultural Festivals: Hosts the annual Congo Square Rhythms Festival and serves as a starting point for parades like the Red Dress Run
attractions.tips: Visit on a Sunday afternoon to witness the traditional drum circles and community dancing. The park is a peaceful escape from the French Quarter's bustle, but be aware that it typically closes at sunset. Look for the historic marker and the 'Dancing in Congo Square' sculpture to fully appreciate its history
attractions.nearby: Louis Armstrong Park, The Mahalia Jackson Theater of the Performing Arts, St. Louis Cemetery No. 1, Backstreet Cultural Museum, French Quarter, Preservation Hall

attractions.name: Congo Square & Louis Armstrong Park
attractions.description: Louis Armstrong Park is a 32-acre urban park located in the historic Tremé neighborhood of New Orleans, just across Rampart Street from the French Quarter. The park honors jazz legend Louis Armstrong and serves as a significant cultural landmark, housing the historic Congo Square where enslaved Africans gathered for musical exchanges. Within its grounds, visitors can find the Mahalia Jackson Theater, the New Orleans Municipal Auditorium, and various sculptures celebrating the city's musical heritage

Iconic 12-foot bronze statue of Louis Armstrong

New Orleans Municipal Auditorium
Sculptures of jazz legends including Charles "Buddy" Bolden and Sidney Bechet

attractions.tips: Visit on Sunday afternoons to experience the historic drum circles and cultural performances in Congo Square. For safety, it is recommended to visit during daylight hours. The iconic white entrance arch is beautifully lit at night and makes for a great photo from Rampart Street. The park is a peaceful retreat and an excellent spot for a picnic away from the busy French Quarter

attractions.name: Dooky Chase's Restaurant
attractions.description: Founded by Emily and Dooky Chase, Sr. in 1941, this Tremé institution is a world-renowned landmark famous for its authentic Creole cuisine and its role as a pivotal meeting place during the Civil Rights Movement. The restaurant is celebrated for its extensive collection of African American art and its legacy of hosting presidents, civil rights leaders, and cultural icons. ()

highlights: Authentic Creole Cuisine including signature Gumbo and Fried Chicken ()

Civil Rights Legacy Walk featuring engraved pavers of historic figures ()

attractions.name: Mahalia Jackson Theater for the Performing Arts
attractions.description: The Mahalia Jackson Theater for the Performing Arts is a premier 2,100-seat venue located in the heart of Louis Armstrong Park. Named after the legendary New Orleans-born gospel singer, the theater serves as the long-term residence for the New Orleans Ballet Association, the New Orleans Opera Association, and Broadway Across America touring productions. Following a significant $30 million refurbishment after Hurricane Katrina, it continues to host a wide range of top-tier talent, including concerts, comedy, and theatrical performances

2,100-seat capacity with diverse seating tiers from Orchestra to Balcony
Named in honor of gospel music icon Mahalia Jackson
Features state-of-the-art sound and lighting systems following a post-Katrina renovation
attractions.tips: Arrive early to enjoy a walk through Louis Armstrong Park before the performance. Note that backpacks and large bags are strictly prohibited, and guests should be prepared for security screening via metal detectors. Tickets can be purchased in person at the Saenger Theatre box office on non-performance days to avoid day-of lines

attractions.name: New Orleans African American Museum
attractions.description: The New Orleans African American Museum of Art, History, and Culture is dedicated to preserving the history and elevating the art, culture, and contributions of African Americans in New Orleans and the African Diaspora. Located in the historic Tremé neighborhood, it is housed on a former plantation site and features several historic structures, including the Meilleur-Goldthwaite House. The museum serves as a cultural nexus for the oldest surviving Black community in the United States

attractions.hours: Thursday–Sunday: 11:00 AM – 4:00 PM; Monday–Wednesday: Closed
attractions.admission: $10 - Student Local Residents; $20 - Adult Local Residents; $15 - Student Global Residents; $25 - Adult Global Residents
highlights: The Katrina List: A living memorial transforming the names of those trapped at the Convention Center into an immersive installation
Ancestral Odyssey by Vince Fraser: A permanent Afro-surrealist digital installation honoring Black Masking Indians
Treme Heroes Mural Project: Commissioned works by contemporary Black artists commemorating legends of the Treme community in the Historic Gardens
Maroon Queen: An ongoing exhibition honoring the legacy of Cherice Harrison-Nelson and Black Masking Indian culture
Everywhere We Are, Everywhere We Go: A permanent historical exhibition delving into the saturated history and contributions of Faubourg Treme
Meilleur-Goldthwaite House: A historic 1829 Creole villa that is the finest remaining master's house in the city
attractions.tips: The museum is currently operating out of its administrative building ("The Blue House") while the historic campus undergoes restoration. It is highly recommended to explore the surrounding Tremé neighborhood, the heart of the oldest Black community in America, and look for the murals on the bridge pillars nearby. Tickets are best purchased in advance, and families should note that many exhibits involve extensive reading

attractions.name: St. Augustine Catholic Church
attractions.description: St. Augustine Catholic Church is the oldest African American Catholic parish in the United States, established in 1841 by free people of color in the historic Tremé neighborhood. It served as a spiritual and cultural anchor for New Orleans' Creole community and remains a vibrant congregation famous for its Sunday Jazz Mass (). The church is also home to the Tomb of the Unknown Slave, a poignant monument honoring the memory of enslaved people ()

attractions.hours: General Visiting Hours: Monday to Saturday, 9:00 AM–5:00 PM; Sunday, 8:00 AM–6:00 PM (). Sunday Mass: 10:00 AM; Wednesday Mass: 5:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Free; donations are encouraged to support restoration and community programs (). Guided tours are available by appointment ()
highlights: Oldest Black Catholic parish in the United States, established in 1841 ())

attractions.tips: Parking is very limited near the church; using public transit or rideshare is highly recommended (). Modest attire is encouraged for services, and visitors should plan to arrive early for the popular Sunday Jazz Mass to secure a seat (). If attending Mass, please stay for the entire service to respect the congregation ()

It is renowned for its labyrinth of ornate above-ground tombs, which were necessitated by the city's high water table. The site serves as a historic time capsule, reflecting centuries of French, Spanish, and Creole cultural and burial traditions

Today, it remains a soulful and authentic community filled with historic landmarks, museums, and colorful Creole architecture. (,)

The area is a "must-visit" for travelers seeking authentic local culture, unique wine bars, and scenic riverfront views at Crescent Park
