attractions.description: Dr. Bob is a self-taught New Orleans folk artist renowned for his signature "Be Nice or Leave" signs and vibrant, eclectic creations made from found objects (). His gallery and studio in the Bywater neighborhood serve as a colorful compound showcasing depictions of local legends, jazz musicians, and swamp creatures (). Visitors can explore the working art factory, meet the artist, and purchase authentic pieces of New Orleans culture ()
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Tuesday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Sunday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Iconic "Be Nice or Leave" signs found throughout the city ()
Art crafted from repurposed materials like bottle caps and architectural salvage ()
Vibrant paintings of local legends, such as the Honey Island Swamp Monster ()
Depictions of New Orleans musical icons like Louis Armstrong and Ernie K-Doe ()
An immersive gallery and "junkyard" studio space located in the heart of Bywater ()
The opportunity to meet Dr. Bob, a genuine New Orleans character ()
attractions.tips: The primary rule is "Be Nice or Leave." Visitors are encouraged to chat with Dr. Bob, who is known for his storytelling (). The studio offers free on-site parking and is a perfect starting point for exploring the Bywater neighborhood's murals (,)
attractions.nearby: Elizabeth's Restaurant, Studio BE, Crescent Park, The Country Club, and Music Box Village (,)

attractions.name: Faubourg Marigny
attractions.description: The Faubourg Marigny is a vibrant and colorful historic neighborhood located just downriver from the French Quarter in New Orleans. Known for its bohemian spirit and artistic soul, it features an exceptionally well-preserved collection of 19th-century architecture, including charming Creole cottages and colorful shotgun homes. The area is celebrated as the city's premier destination for authentic live jazz, centered around the world-famous clubs and street performers of Frenchmen Street
attractions.address: 700 Elysian Fields Ave (Washington Square Park), New Orleans, LA 70117 (Neighborhood boundaries: Esplanade Ave, St. Claude Ave, Homer Plessy Way, and the Mississippi River)
attractions.hours: Open 24/7 (Individual business and park hours vary; Washington Square Park is typically open 8:00 AM – 7:00 PM)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Frenchmen Street: A world-renowned live music district featuring iconic jazz clubs like Snug Harbor and The Spotted Cat
Washington Square Park: A beautiful, oak-shaded historic square that serves as a community hub for festivals and relaxation
Creole Architecture: One of the most intact collections of 19th-century Creole cottages and Victorian shotgun houses in the United States
Marigny Opera House: A former 1850s church turned performing arts center known for its stunning architecture and community performances
St. Roch Market: A historic public market building reborn as a trendy food hall featuring diverse local culinary vendors
Crescent Park: A modern 1.4-mile linear park along the Mississippi River offering unparalleled views of the city skyline
attractions.tips: The Marigny is best explored on foot or by bicycle to enjoy its compact, pedestrian-friendly streets and vibrant house colors. For the best music experience, visit Frenchmen Street in the evening, and don't miss the nightly open-air Frenchmen Art Market for unique local crafts. Consider visiting on the second Saturday of the month for the local art walk when many neighborhood galleries open their doors to the public
attractions.nearby: French Quarter, Bywater, St. Roch, Mississippi River, New Orleans Jazz Museum

attractions.name: Frenchmen Art Bazaar
attractions.description: The Frenchmen Art Bazaar is a vibrant, open-air night market located in the heart of New Orleans' famous Frenchmen Street music district. It features a revolving showcase of over 100 local and regional artists offering handmade goods such as jewelry, paintings, and mixed media in a whimsical atmosphere. Visitors can browse unique souvenirs while enjoying the live music and lively street energy of the Marigny neighborhood
attractions.hours: Monday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Tuesday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Wednesday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Thursday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Friday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Saturday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM, Sunday: 6:00 PM – 12:00 AM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Nighttime art market featuring handmade local goods
Over 100 rotating artists and makers
Selection of jewelry, sculpture, and original paintings
Whimsical open-air setting with outdoor lounges
Family-friendly atmosphere amidst jazz clubs
Interactive art experiences and vendor demos
attractions.tips: Visit later in the evening to experience the full energy of Frenchmen Street. Talk to the artists to hear their stories and find one-of-a-kind handmade souvenirs. The market is located in a long alley-like space next to The Spotted Cat, making it a perfect stop between jazz sets
attractions.nearby: The Spotted Cat Music Club, Snug Harbor Jazz Bistro, Washington Square Park, New Orleans Jazz Museum

attractions.name: Frenchmen Street
attractions.description: Frenchmen Street is a vibrant three-block stretch in the Faubourg Marigny neighborhood, renowned as the premier destination for authentic live music in New Orleans. It features a high density of jazz, blues, and reggae clubs, alongside late-night eateries and local art markets. Originally a local favorite, it has gained international fame for its lively atmosphere and rich cultural heritage
attractions.address: 500-600 Blocks of Frenchmen St, New Orleans, LA 70116
attractions.hours: Street access: 24/7. Clubs typically open around 4:00 PM - 7:00 PM daily; Music sets usually run from 5:00 PM or 9:00 PM until late. Frenchmen Art Bazaar: Sun-Wed 7:00 PM-12:00 AM, Thu-Sat 7:00 PM-1:00 AM
attractions.admission: Walking the street is Free; individual music clubs may have cover charges that vary by venue and performer
highlights: High concentration of live music venues including Snug Harbor and The Spotted Cat
Nightly Frenchmen Art Bazaar featuring local artists
Authentic local atmosphere often preferred over Bourbon Street
Historic architecture including Creole cottages and townhouses
Diverse late-night dining options and record stores
Frequent street performances and brass bands
attractions.tips: Arrive early (before 9:00 PM) to get a seat in smaller clubs. Carry cash for cover charges and tipping musicians. While most clubs are 21+, some like The Maison and 30/90 allow minors until 10:00 PM
attractions.nearby: French Quarter, New Orleans Jazz Museum at the Old U.S. Mint, Washington Square Park, Esplanade Avenue

attractions.name: Holy Cross Neighborhood (Holy Cross Historic District)
attractions.description: Holy Cross is a historic, tranquil neighborhood in New Orleans' Lower Ninth Ward, characterized by its high ground and diverse architecture including shotgun houses and Creole cottages. It is most famous for the iconic 'Steamboat Gothic' Doullut Houses and offers residents a strong sense of community and breathtaking views of the Mississippi River. The area is a National Register Historic District known for its resilient spirit and commitment to sustainable rebuilding
attractions.address: 5130 Chartres St, New Orleans, LA 70117 (Neighborhood Association / CSED) / 400 Egania St (Doullut Steamboat House)
attractions.hours: Open 24/7
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Doullut Steamboat Houses
Mississippi River Levee walking path with views of the downtown skyline
Historic Holy Cross School original campus site
Delery Street Riverfront Playground
Abundant historic architecture including Creole cottages and Queen Anne estates
attractions.tips: Take a walk or bike ride along the levee for the best views of the river and city skyline; the Doullut Steamboat Houses are private residences, so please view them from the public sidewalk or levee; the neighborhood is less touristy than the French Quarter, so expect a quiet, residential atmosphere
attractions.nearby: Marigny/Bywater, Jackson Barracks, House of Dance & Feathers, Lower Ninth Ward Living Museum

attractions.name: St. Roch Cemetery No. 1 & No. 2 ()
attractions.description: Established in 1874 by Reverend Peter Leonard Thevis, St. Roch is a historic above-ground cemetery known as a site of pilgrimage and healing (). Its focal point is a Gothic-style mortuary chapel dedicated to the patron saint of good health, featuring a unique collection of ex-votos like prosthetic limbs and glass eyes left by the faithful (,). The cemetery exemplifies the traditional "Cities of the Dead" architecture with masonry tombs and wall vaults ()
attractions.address: 1725 St. Roch Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70117 (,)
attractions.hours: Daily 8:30 AM – 4:00 PM; Chapel visitation is generally the first Friday of each month from 11:00 AM – Noon (,)
attractions.admission: Free (,)
highlights: National Shrine of St. Roch and its Gothic-style mortuary chapel ()
Unique collection of ex-votos (crutches, braces, doll parts) left by those seeking healing ()
14 Stations of the Cross statuary carved in Italy by Enrico Arrighini ()
Above-ground masonry tombs and traditional wall vaults ()
The final resting place of cemetery founder Rev. Peter Leonard Thevis ()
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes for uneven gravel paths, and visit on weekday mornings for a peaceful experience (). Be aware that bike tours and motorized vehicles are prohibited, and pets must be kept leashed at all times ()
attractions.nearby: St. Roch Market, Marigny Opera House, Frenchmen Street music clubs, St. Roch Park (,)

attractions.name: The Spotted Cat Music Club
attractions.description: The Spotted Cat Music Club is a legendary jazz venue located on Frenchmen Street in the heart of the Faubourg Marigny district. Internationally recognized as a top destination for live music, it features local talent performing jazz, blues, funk, and klezmer in an intimate, authentic atmosphere (). The club is a local favorite and has been featured in numerous films and media publications ()
attractions.hours: Monday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Tuesday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Wednesday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Thursday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Friday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Saturday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM, Sunday: 2:00 PM - 2:00 AM (,)
attractions.days_open: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday ()
attractions.admission: Varies; generally no cover charge Sunday through Thursday, with a $5 cover charge on Friday and Saturday evenings (,). A one-drink minimum per set is required for all patrons ()
highlights: Quintessential New Orleans jazz club atmosphere ()
Live performances daily featuring Traditional and Modern Jazz, Blues, and Funk ()
Internationally acclaimed as a TOP 150 Jazz Club ()
Prime location on the famous Frenchmen Street music corridor ()
Features a diverse rotation of renowned local bands and musicians ()
Cozy, intimate space that offers a "locals" music experience ()
attractions.tips: The club is strictly 21 and over; a valid US photo ID or foreign passport is required for entry (). Since the venue is small and mostly standing-room only, it is recommended to arrive 10-15 minutes before a set to secure a spot (). Be sure to bring cash for tipping the musicians, and keep in mind that there is a one-drink minimum per set ()

attractions.name: Three Muses
attractions.description: Three Muses is an intimate venue on Frenchmen Street that combines live local jazz with a menu of craft cocktails and American comfort food featuring a Korean twist. The establishment is known for its "chic-yet-affordable" small plates and soulful atmosphere, offering a quintessential New Orleans "dinner and a show" experience
attractions.hours: Monday: 7:00 PM - 11:00 PM; Tuesday: 7:00 PM - 11:00 PM; Wednesday: 7:00 PM - 11:00 PM; Thursday: 7:00 PM - 11:00 PM; Friday: 7:00 PM - 11:00 PM; Saturday: 6:00 PM - 12:00 AM; Sunday: 4:30 PM - 12:00 AM (Note: Live music often begins earlier than standard dinner hours; check schedule for specific set times)
attractions.days_open: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Free entry; a $3 per person live music fee is added to all dine-in checks to support local musicians
highlights: Live local jazz, swing, and brass music daily
Asian-influenced comfort food such as Beef Bulgogi and Korean Fried Chicken Sandwiches
Artisanal and signature craft cocktails
Intimate, small-plate dining and tapas-style menu
Located in the heart of the Frenchmen Street music district
Lively, local-friendly atmosphere away from the main Bourbon Street crowds
attractions.tips: Arrive early as the venue is small and operates on a first-come, first-served basis with no reservations. Be aware that the restaurant does not provide separate checks for groups. For the best experience, try the signature Ms. Moon's Eggrolls or the seasoned tater tots while enjoying the music
attractions.nearby: The Spotted Cat Music Club, d.b.a., Blue Nile, Snug Harbor Jazz Bistro, French Market, Washington Square Park

attractions.name: d.b.a. New Orleans
attractions.category: Jazz Club, Live Music Venue, and Bar
attractions.description: Established in 2000, d.b.a. is a legendary live music club and bar located in the heart of Frenchmen Street. It offers a vast selection of beer, wine, and spirits in a comfortable, smoke-free setting, featuring local and regional acts nightly. The venue is renowned for its rich history, unique wooden ambiance, and contribution to the vibrant New Orleans music scene
attractions.hours: Monday-Friday: 6:00 PM - 1:00 AM; Saturday: 2:00 PM - 1:00 AM; Sunday: 6:00 PM - 1:00 AM
attractions.admission: Varies; often features $20 advance tickets, $10 cover for Treme Brass Band, and some shows are free with no cover
highlights: Nightly live music featuring local and regional jazz, brass, and funk acts
Extensive selection of hundreds of craft beers, wines, and spirits
Smoke-free environment for a comfortable listening experience
Legendary Tuesday night performances by the Treme Brass Band
Intimate venue with a warm, all-wood interior providing excellent acoustics
Recently under new ownership as of late 2023, embarking on a 'New Chapter'
attractions.tips: Seating is limited and mostly along the walls, so arrive early if you need a seat or wear comfortable shoes for standing. The venue is strictly 21 and over. Cover charges are typically paid at the door, but advance tickets for certain shows can be found online
attractions.nearby: The Spotted Cat Music Club, Snug Harbor Jazz Bistro, Washington Square Park, New Orleans Jazz Museum, and various other clubs and restaurants along Frenchmen Street

attractions.name: Audubon Park
attractions.description: Audubon Park is a historic 350-acre municipal park located in the Uptown neighborhood of New Orleans, situated between the Mississippi River and St. Charles Avenue. It features a lush landscape characterized by century-old live oaks, a scenic lagoon, and a popular 1.8-mile paved trail for recreation. The park is a central hub for outdoor activity and is home to the Audubon Zoo and the waterfront area known as "The Fly.")
attractions.hours: Monday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Tuesday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Wednesday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Thursday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Friday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Saturday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM, Sunday: 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free (Note: Fees apply for the Golf Club, Tennis courts, and the Audubon Zoo)
highlights: 1.8-mile paved loop for walking, jogging, and cycling
Bird Island (Ochsner Island) bird sanctuary in the central lagoon
"The Fly" waterfront area with views of the Mississippi River
Audubon Park Golf Club, an 18-hole executive course
Historic Live Oaks draped in Spanish moss
Multiple playgrounds and picnic pavilions
attractions.tips: Take the St. Charles Streetcar for a scenic ride to the park entrance. Visit the waterfront area known as "The Fly" behind the zoo for excellent sunset views over the Mississippi River. The park is highly bike-friendly, and bringing a picnic to enjoy under the historic oaks is a local favorite
attractions.nearby: Audubon Zoo, Tulane University, Loyola University, Magazine Street shops, St. Charles Avenue

attractions.name: Audubon Zoo
attractions.description: Located in historic Uptown New Orleans, Audubon Zoo is a world-renowned 58-acre facility featuring exotic and endangered animals in award-winning natural habitats. It is home to over 2,000 animals representing more than 350 species from across the world. Visitors can explore unique areas like the Louisiana Swamp and the Jaguar Jungle, making it a top family attraction in the United States
attractions.hours: Monday – Sunday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adults: $30–$40; Children (2-12) & Seniors (65+): $25–$35; Children under 2: Free; Audubon Members: Free
highlights: Louisiana Swamp exhibit featuring rare white alligators
Jaguar Jungle's River’s Edge with spider monkeys and Mesoamerican wildlife
African lion cub trio: Nandi, Zawadi, and Tatu
Cool Zoo seasonal waterpark with a lazy river and slides
Historic Swamp Train rides through the architectural grounds
Asian elephant and giraffe habitats
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets online in advance to secure entry and skip lines; download the Audubon Zoo app for a digital guide and map; pack swimsuits if visiting during the season for the Cool Zoo waterpark
attractions.nearby: Audubon Park, The Fly (Riverview area), Tulane University, Loyola University New Orleans

attractions.name: Brigtsen's Restaurant
attractions.description: Housed in a converted Victorian cottage in the Riverbend area, Brigtsen's Restaurant has been showcasing sophisticated interpretations of Louisiana cuisine since 1986 (). Owned and operated by James Beard Award-winner Chef Frank Brigtsen and his wife Marna, the establishment is known for its warm, homey atmosphere and exceptional service (). The menu features creative Cajun and Creole dishes, such as butternut shrimp bisque and a renowned seafood platter, emphasizing local ingredients and authentic flavors ()
attractions.hours: Tuesday - Thursday: 5:00 PM – 8:30 PM (last reservation); Friday - Saturday: 5:00 PM – 9:30 PM (last reservation); Sunday - Monday: Closed ()
attractions.admission: Price varies; main courses typically range from $28.00 to $48.00 ()
highlights: James Beard Award-winning Chef Frank Brigtsen ()
Located in a charming renovated Victorian cottage in the Riverbend neighborhood ()
Signature dish: Butternut Shrimp Bisque ()
Famous Pecan Pie served with caramel sauce and whipped cream ()
Intimate, homey dining rooms with personalized, "old school" service ()
Consistently voted Top Cajun Restaurant and Top Service in the Zagat Survey ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended and are only accepted by telephone during business hours (). The dress code is business casual, and a 3.75% surcharge applies to credit card transactions (,). Arriving early allows for a peaceful stroll through the surrounding Riverbend neighborhood or along the river ()
attractions.nearby: Audubon Park and Audubon Zoo (), St. Charles Streetcar Line (), the Mississippi River banks (), and the historic Camellia Grill ()

attractions.name: Clancy's Restaurant
attractions.description: Tucked away on a residential corner in the Uptown neighborhood, Clancy's is a quintessential New Orleans institution celebrated for its refined Creole cuisine and sophisticated yet lively atmosphere. Originally a neighborhood po'boy shop, the restaurant has evolved into a premier fine-dining destination known for its signature dishes like fried oysters with brie and smoked duck. It remains a local favorite, offering a professional white-tablecloth experience with an extensive, highly-regarded wine list
attractions.hours: Tue: 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Wed: 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Thu: 11:30 AM – 2:00 PM, 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Fri: 11:30 AM – 2:00 PM, 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Sat: 5:00 PM – 10:00 PM; Sun: Closed; Mon: Closed
attractions.admission: Varies
highlights: Signature Fried Oysters with Brie
Famous Smoked Duck and Smoked Soft-shell Crab
Decadent Lemon Icebox Pie for dessert
Extensive wine list with a focus on white Burgundies
White-tablecloth dining in a lively neighborhood setting
MICHELIN-recognized Creole cuisine
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended and can be made up to one month in advance via Resy or by phone. The restaurant is known for its high energy and can be quite loud when full. Don't miss the peppermint ice cream or the signature lemon icebox pie to round out your meal
attractions.nearby: Audubon Park, Audubon Zoo, Dos Jefes Uptown Cigar Bar, St. Charles Avenue Streetcar

attractions.name: Cure
attractions.description: Cure is a James Beard Award-winning cocktail bar located in a beautifully restored 1905 horse and carriage firehouse in Uptown New Orleans. Opened in 2009, it is widely credited with pioneering the city's modern craft cocktail movement and revitalizing the Freret Street corridor. The bar features a sophisticated, dimly-lit atmosphere with a library-style back bar and a focus on both innovative seasonal drinks and high-end classics
attractions.hours: Friday & Saturday: 3:00 PM – 12:00 AM; Sunday – Thursday: 4:00 PM – 11:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 2018 James Beard Award winner for Outstanding Bar Program ()
Ranked among North America's 50 Best Bars ()
Housed in a historic, renovated 1905 firehouse with original brick walls ()
Extensive spirits selection specializing in rare American whiskeys ()
Rotating menu of seasonal cocktails attributed to their individual creators ()
Pioneering establishment of the New Orleans craft cocktail revival ()
attractions.tips: Take advantage of the daily happy hour (3–6 PM Fri–Sat, 4–6 PM Sun–Thu) for $8 classic cocktails and wine (). Try the house favorite "daily punch" and pair it with small plates like the smoked trout dip or house-made pimento cheese (,). Reservations are recommended, especially for weekend evenings ()
attractions.nearby: Tulane and Loyola Universities, Gasa Gasa, Dat Dog, High Hat Cafe, Bearcat Cafe ()

attractions.name: Domilise's Po-Boy & Bar
attractions.description: Established in 1918, Domilise's is a legendary family-owned Uptown institution recognized as a standard-bearer for authentic New Orleans po-boys. The restaurant is housed in a small, iconic yellow house and is famous for its fried seafood and roast beef sandwiches served on fresh Leidenheimer bread. It has earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand and has been featured on numerous national food programs for its quality and historical significance
attractions.hours: Monday: 11:00 AM - 3:00 PM; Tuesday: 11:00 AM - 3:00 PM; Wednesday: 11:00 AM - 3:00 PM; Thursday: 11:00 AM - 5:00 PM; Friday: 11:00 AM - 7:00 PM; Saturday: 11:00 AM - 7:00 PM; Sunday: Closed
attractions.admission: Varies (menu prices)
highlights: Traditional po-boys served on fresh, crispy Leidenheimer bread
Signature "Peacemaker" po-boy featuring half fried shrimp and half fried oysters
Family-owned and operated since 1918, now in its third and fourth generations
Michelin Bib Gourmand recipient and featured on "Adam Richman's Best Sandwich in America"
Historic neighborhood "hole-in-the-wall" atmosphere with a classic wooden bar
Famous roast beef po-boy with rich gravy and Swiss cheese
attractions.tips: Be prepared for a line, especially during peak lunch hours as the shop is popular with both locals and tourists. The ordering system involves placing your order at the counter and waiting for your name to be called; seating is limited, so many patrons grab a drink at the bar while waiting. The shop is located in a residential area, so it offers a more "off the beaten path" experience compared to downtown eateries
attractions.nearby: Tipitina's, Audubon Park, Audubon Zoo, Magazine Street Shopping District, Hansen's Sno-Bliz

attractions.name: Loyola University New Orleans
attractions.description: Loyola University New Orleans is a private Jesuit university located in the heart of the historic Uptown neighborhood. Established in 1904, it offers a holistic education focusing on the whole person—mind, body, and spirit—to prepare students for lives of service and justice. The campus is situated on St. Charles Avenue, directly across from Audubon Park, and is known for its beautiful Gothic-style architecture
attractions.address: 6363 St. Charles Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70118
attractions.hours: Administrative Offices: Monday - Friday: 8:30 a.m. – 4:45 p.m.; Saturday - Sunday: Closed. Campus grounds are generally accessible daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic Marquette Hall, the oldest building on campus completed in 1910
Award-winning J. Edgar and Louise S. Monroe Library
Strong Jesuit identity and commitment to social justice
Located on the historic St. Charles Streetcar line
Over 100 student organizations, clubs, and intramural sports
attractions.tips: Schedule a guided on-campus tour at least 48 hours in advance for a comprehensive experience. Use the historic St. Charles Streetcar for a scenic commute to the campus. Visitors can find free parking in the Marquette Horseshoe on St. Charles Avenue. Explore the Broadway campus to see the historic Greenville Hall
attractions.nearby: Audubon Park and Zoo, Tulane University, Holy Name of Jesus Catholic Church, Camellia Grill

attractions.name: Magazine Street
attractions.description: Magazine Street is a premier six-mile thoroughfare in New Orleans known for its eclectic mix of local boutiques, antique shops, art galleries, and diverse dining options. It stretches through several historic neighborhoods, offering a more local-oriented experience compared to the French Quarter, with architecture ranging from grand mansions to Victorian row houses
attractions.address: Magazine St (stretching from Canal St to Audubon Park), New Orleans, LA 70115
attractions.hours: Daily: Most shops open 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM/6:00 PM; Restaurants vary with many serving breakfast, lunch, and dinner. Sunday hours often start later at noon
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Six miles of diverse shopping, dining, and art experiences
Home to a wide array of local boutiques and world-renowned antique shops
Features historic architecture including mansions and Victorian shotgun houses
Host to annual marquee events like the Champagne Stroll and Art Walk
Direct access to major attractions like the Audubon Zoo at its terminus
attractions.tips: Start your visit early as many shops close by 5:00 PM or 6:00 PM. The section between Washington Ave and Napoleon Ave is particularly popular for concentrated shopping and dining. Consider taking the St. Charles Streetcar and walking a few blocks over to access different sections of the street
attractions.nearby: Audubon Zoo, National WWII Museum, Garden District, Lafayette Cemetery No. 1, St. Charles Streetcar Line, Sazerac House

attractions.name: Reconcile New Orleans, Inc. / Café Reconcile ()
attractions.description: Café Reconcile is a non-profit workforce training restaurant that provides life skills and hospitality training to young adults ages 16-24 in the Central City neighborhood. The café serves authentic New Orleans "soul food" and Creole favorites, offering a menu that includes fried catfish, smothered turkey necks, and shrimp po' boys. It serves as a cornerstone for community rehabilitation, with all dining proceeds supporting its student-centered mission (,)
attractions.address: 1631 Oretha Castle Haley Blvd, New Orleans, LA 70113 ()
attractions.hours: Tuesday – Friday: 11:00 AM – 2:30 PM; Saturday – Monday: Closed ()
attractions.admission: Free; menu prices vary ()
highlights: Workforce development program for at-risk youth providing culinary and life skills training ()
Authentic New Orleans soul food classics such as fried catfish, gumbo, and shrimp po' boys ()
Famous Bananas Foster Bread Pudding dessert ()
Located in a historic five-story building that serves as a cornerstone of Central City's revival ()
Consistently recognized as a top dining destination by critics from the New York Times and Eater ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are strongly recommended via OpenTable due to limited lunch-only hours. Any tip left by guests is considered a tax-deductible donation that directly supports the non-profit's training program. The restaurant offers free 2-hour secure parking for diners (,)
attractions.nearby: Southern Food & Beverage Museum, Ashé Cultural Arts Center, New Orleans Jazz Market, and the George and Leah McKenna Museum of African American Art ()

attractions.name: Shaya ()
attractions.description: Shaya is a James Beard Award-winning restaurant in New Orleans that specializes in modern Israeli cuisine. It blends culinary traditions from the Middle East, North Africa, and Eastern Europe with local Louisiana ingredients. The restaurant is renowned for its wood-fired oven, which produces pillowy, hand-crafted pita bread served alongside a variety of seasonal hummus and small plates (,)
attractions.hours: Sunday–Thursday: 11:00 am–9:00 pm; Friday–Saturday: 11:00 am–10:00 pm; Weekday Happy Hour: Monday–Friday 4:00 pm–6:00 pm ()
attractions.admission: Varies; small plates and hummus are approximately $15-$20, while larger entrees range up to $54 (,)
highlights: James Beard Award-winning modern Israeli cuisine ()
Wood-fired oven for fresh, hand-crafted pita bread ()
Menu features a blend of Middle Eastern, North African, and Turkish influences ()
Focus on locally and sustainably sourced Louisiana ingredients ()
Stylish atmosphere with an outdoor courtyard and private event spaces ()
Communal dining style perfect for sharing small plates and salatim ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended due to high popularity. Don't miss the wood-fired pita and seasonal hummus selections. Visit during the weekday happy hour for discounts on specialty hummus and wine (,)
attractions.nearby: La Petite Grocery, Superior Seafood & Oyster Bar, Creole Creamery, Magazine Street shopping, Tipitina's ()

attractions.name: Southern Food & Beverage Museum
attractions.description: The Southern Food & Beverage Museum is a non-profit living history organization dedicated to the discovery, understanding, and celebration of the food and drink culture of the American South. Located in the historic Dryades Market, the museum explores the intersection between culture and food through interactive exhibits and programming. It also houses the Museum of the American Cocktail (MOTAC) and the SoFAB Research Center. (,)
attractions.address: 1504 Oretha Castle Haley Blvd, New Orleans, LA 70113
attractions.hours: Friday: 11:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Saturday: 11:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Sunday: 11:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Monday: 11:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Tuesday: Closed, Wednesday: Closed, Thursday: Closed ()
attractions.admission: $12 Adults (21+), $10 Seniors (60+), $10 Students (with ID), $10 Military (with ID), Children under 12 Free (,)
highlights: Museum of the American Cocktail (MOTAC) featuring rare spirits and bar tools ()
Deelightful Roux School of Cooking for hands-on culinary classes ()
Lahe Chase Louisiana Gallery focusing on regional creole cuisine ()
Gumbo Garden with plants from Africa, Europe, and North America ()
Culinary Heritage Sign Gallery featuring historic food-related signage ()
Galerie d'Absinthe showcasing the history and artifacts of the 'Green Fairy' ()
attractions.tips: Plan for 1-2 hours to fully explore the exhibits. Booking a cooking class in advance is highly recommended as they are a highlight and often sell out. Free street parking is typically available nearby. ()
attractions.nearby: Ashé Cultural Arts Center, Café Reconcile, Maïs Arepas, Ogden Museum of Southern Art, Contemporary Arts Center (,)

attractions.name: The Camellia Grill
attractions.description: The Camellia Grill is a fabled landmark diner located in the Uptown neighborhood of New Orleans. Since opening in 1946, it has become a cherished institution famous for its classic diner fare, professional bow-tied servers, and vibrant communal atmosphere. It is widely recognized as a cultural melting pot where tradition is served with every meal
attractions.address: 626 S Carrollton Ave, New Orleans, LA 70118

highlights: Classic hand-made burgers
World-famous omelets
Signature chocolate freezes
Professional bow-tied servers
Historic counter-style seating
Grilled pecan pie with ice cream
attractions.tips: Take the historic St. Charles Streetcar to the end of the line at Carrollton Avenue for a classic New Orleans experience. Be prepared for counter seating and potential wait times during peak breakfast and brunch hours, as this is a popular spot for both locals and tourists. Don't forget to try the pecan pie, which is famously grilled on the flat-top
attractions.nearby: St. Charles Streetcar Line, Audubon Park, Audubon Zoo, Oak Street shopping and dining district (Maple Leaf Bar, Jacques-Imo's), Cooter Brown's Tavern

attractions.name: Tulane University
attractions.description: Tulane University is a private research university in New Orleans, Louisiana, founded in 1834 as the Medical College of Louisiana. It is one of the most prestigious universities in the Southern United States, known for its historic architecture and beautiful 110-acre campus. The university is a member of the Association of American Universities and is classified among R1: Doctoral Universities with very high research activity
attractions.address: 6823 St. Charles Avenue, New Orleans, LA 70118
attractions.hours: Campus: Open 24/7; Admission Tours: Weekdays at 9:00 AM & 2:00 PM, Most Saturdays at 9:00 AM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic Gibson Hall and the iconic limestone buildings on St. Charles Avenue
The legendary Mardi Gras Bead Tree
Newcomb Art Museum, featuring socially engaged art and original exhibitions
Middle American Research Institute (MARI) museum of Mayan history
Scenic live oak trees and lush green spaces across the uptown campus
attractions.tips: Arrive via the historic St. Charles Streetcar for a scenic view of the Garden District and Uptown mansions. The campus is directly across from Audubon Park, making it easy to combine a campus stroll with a park visit. If you want a guided tour, be sure to book in advance through the undergraduate admission portal
attractions.nearby: Audubon Park, Audubon Zoo, Magazine Street, Loyola University, Freret Street

attractions.name: Upperline Restaurant (,)
attractions.description: Upperline Restaurant was a celebrated fine dining establishment in New Orleans, housed in a charming 1877 yellow townhouse. It was renowned for its unique blend of Cajun and Creole cuisine, vibrant local art collection, and warm hospitality provided by owner JoAnn Clevenger. The restaurant was a local favorite for decades before its permanent closure following the COVID-19 pandemic (,)
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Thursday: 5:30 PM – 9:00 PM; Friday–Saturday: 5:30 PM – 9:30 PM; Sunday: 5:30 PM – 8:00 PM; Monday–Tuesday: Closed (,)
attractions.admission: Varies; typical fine dining prices with three-course dinner options around $45 (,)
highlights: Signature dish: Fried Green Tomatoes with Shrimp Remoulade ()
Extensive collection of over 400 local artworks ()
Housed in a historic yellow townhouse built in 1877 ()
Wine Spectator Award-winning wine list ()
Famous "Taste of New Orleans" seven-item tasting menu ()
Featured on Food Network's 'The Best Thing I Ever Ate' for its roast duck ()
attractions.tips: Reservations were highly recommended as it was a popular destination for both locals and celebrities. The dress code was generally "nice casual," though many guests opted for slightly more formal attire to match the fine dining atmosphere (,)

attractions.name: Uptown New Orleans
attractions.description: Uptown is a large, primarily residential section of New Orleans settled in the 19th century, known for its historic mansions, lush oak-lined streets, and vibrant commercial corridors. As the city's largest National Register Historic District, it encompasses over 10,000 significant historic buildings and is famous for the iconic St. Charles Avenue streetcar line. The area offers a blend of majestic architecture, local shops, and prestigious universities like Tulane and Loyola
attractions.address: Bounded by LaSalle St (N), Napoleon Ave (E), Magazine St (S), and Jefferson Ave (W)
attractions.hours: Neighborhood: 24/7; St. Charles Streetcar: 24/7
attractions.admission: Free (neighborhood access); St. Charles Streetcar: $1.25 per ride or $3 for a 1-day Jazzy Pass
highlights: St. Charles Avenue Streetcar
Magazine Street shopping and dining corridor
Audubon Park and Audubon Zoo
Tulane and Loyola Universities
Historic mansions and 19th-century architecture
Lafayette Cemetery No. 1 and Garden District area
attractions.tips: The best way to see the neighborhood is via the St. Charles Avenue streetcar; bring exact change or use the Le Pass app. Magazine Street is ideal for walking and exploring local boutiques and eateries. Many areas are very safe, but be cautious on dimly lit side streets at night. Check out the Freret Street corridor for a modern local vibe
attractions.nearby: Garden District, Carrollton, Irish Channel, Audubon Park, Central Business District

attractions.name: Bayou St. John
attractions.description: Bayou St. John is a historic and picturesque neighborhood in New Orleans centered around a scenic waterway that once served as a vital trade route. Known for its tranquil atmosphere, it is a popular spot for outdoor activities like kayaking, picnicking, and watching the sunset. The area is rich in history, featuring some of the city's oldest landmarks and hosting major cultural events like the Bayou Boogaloo festival. (,)
attractions.address: Moss St & Esplanade Ave, New Orleans, LA 70119
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (Neighborhood); Waterway banks typically 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Kayaking and paddleboarding on the historic waterway
The Pitot House, a circa-1799 West Indies-style plantation home
Cabrini (Magnolia) Bridge, a popular spot for picnics and sunset views
Annual festivals such as Bayou Boogaloo and Praise Fest
Historic St. Louis Cemetery No. 3 with its unique above-ground tombs
Proximity to the Lafitte Greenway for walking and biking
attractions.tips: Grab a po-boy from the nearby Parkway Bakery & Tavern for a classic bayou picnic. For a unique perspective, rent a kayak to see the historic homes along Moss Street from the water. The Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden is just across the bayou and offers free admission. (,)
attractions.nearby: New Orleans City Park, New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden, Fair Grounds Race Course, and the Lafitte Greenway. ()

attractions.name: Big Lake at New Orleans City Park
attractions.description: Big Lake is a premier 25-acre recreational destination within New Orleans City Park, featuring a three-quarter-mile scenic trail for walking, jogging, and biking. The area is enriched with art installations, native plant trails, and diverse wildlife, offering a peaceful escape for both locals and visitors. It is a popular spot for birdwatching and enjoying the water via boat rentals or authentic gondola rides
attractions.hours: Park Grounds: Sunrise to Sunset daily; Boat/Bike Rentals: 10am–Sunset daily, Night Rides: Sunset–9pm daily
attractions.admission: Free entry to the park and trails; boat rentals $16-$38/hr, bike rentals $9-$40/hr, Venetian gondola tours $100 for 2 people
highlights: Zemurray Multi-Use Trail
The Singing Oak wind chime installation
Swan boat, kayak, and canoe rentals
Authentic Venetian gondola tours
Big Lake Native Plant Trail
Illuminated swan boat night rides
attractions.tips: Visit at sunset for illuminated swan boat rides and bring a photo ID for all rentals. Shoreline fishing is permitted, but fishing from boats is prohibited. Friends of City Park members can enjoy a 20% discount on boat and bike rentals

attractions.description: Carousel Gardens Amusement Park is a historic, family-friendly destination located within New Orleans City Park, featuring 18 charming attractions and two cafés. It is home to the world-renowned "Flying Horses," one of the oldest hand-carved wooden carousels in the United States. The park offers a nostalgic atmosphere with classic rides like the Ladybug Rollercoaster and a miniature train that tours the park grounds. (,)

Ladybug Rollercoaster
Miniature Train Ride around the park
City Park Ferris Wheel
Bumper Cars and Tilt-A-Whirl
Storyland

Wearing comfortable shoes is essential for navigating the park and adjacent attractions. (,)

attractions.name: Katie's Restaurant & Bar
attractions.description: Katie’s is a family-owned neighborhood staple in Mid-City, New Orleans, serving scratch-made Creole comfort food since 1984. Rebuilt and reopened in 2010 after being destroyed by Hurricane Katrina, the restaurant is known for its warm hospitality and diverse menu that features local favorites and creative dishes. It offers a cozy atmosphere where regulars and tourists alike enjoy classic New Orleans flavors and innovative cocktails

"The Barge," a massive 3-foot seafood po-boy
Award-winning Crawfish Beignets
Outdoor patio seating and "Sunday Funday" brunch

attractions.name: Lake Pontchartrain / New Canal Lighthouse Museum
attractions.description: Lake Pontchartrain is a vast 630-square-mile estuary in southeastern Louisiana, known as the largest inland body of water in the state. It serves as a vital ecological and recreational hub for New Orleans, featuring the iconic 23-mile Causeway Bridge and the historic New Canal Lighthouse. Visitors can enjoy activities ranging from boating and fishing to exploring the lake's rich history and environmental importance at the local museum
attractions.hours: Tuesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Sunday: Closed (except first Sunday for special events), Monday: Closed

highlights: Home to the Lake Pontchartrain Causeway, the world's longest bridge over water
Features the New Canal Lighthouse Museum & Education Center
Abundant opportunities for fishing, boating, and birdwatching
Brackish water ecosystem supporting diverse wildlife like blue crabs and redfish
Scenic Lakeshore Drive for biking and walking
attractions.tips: Visit the New Canal Lighthouse on the first Sunday of the month for free admission. For the best views of the lake, take a drive across the Causeway Bridge or enjoy a sunset at one of the lakefront restaurants like The Blue Crab

attractions.name: Lakeview
attractions.description: Lakeview is a charming residential neighborhood in New Orleans situated between the shores of Lake Pontchartrain and the expansive City Park. Developed largely after World War II, it is known for its mid-century architecture, family-friendly atmosphere, and vibrant boating community. The neighborhood's central hub, Harrison Avenue, features a diverse array of stylish boutiques, local cafes, and renowned seafood restaurants

highlights: Proximity to Lake Pontchartrain and active marinas for sailing and fishing
Home to Harrison Avenue, a major corridor for local dining and shopping
Adjacent to City Park, offering easy access to the New Orleans Museum of Art and Besthoff Sculpture Garden
Known for its relaxed, carefree vibe and homey residential architecture
Host to popular seasonal events such as Greek Fest and the Harrison Avenue Stroll
Historical significance related to the New Basin Canal development
attractions.tips: It is highly recommended to explore the neighborhood by car, as public transit is limited in this area compared to the city center. Be sure to visit the Lakefront area for sunset views over the water and fresh seafood at local staples. For a local experience, check the community calendar for the 'Harrison Avenue Stroll' events

attractions.name: Longue Vue House and Gardens
attractions.category: Historic House Museum and Gardens / Landmark
attractions.description: Longue Vue House and Gardens is a multifaceted historic estate featuring a world-class house museum and eight acres of stunning gardens. Formerly the residence of philanthropists Edgar and Edith Stern, this National Historic Landmark showcases a magnificent Classical Revival mansion and a visionary landscape design by Ellen Biddle Shipman. Visitors can explore themed garden rooms, original 1940s furnishings, and an unparalleled collection of Louisiana irises
attractions.hours: Gardens & Cafe: Mon – Sun, 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM; House Tours: Daily at 10:00 AM, 11:30 AM, 1:00 PM, 2:30 PM, and 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Gardens: Adults $15 advance / $17 arrival, Children $10/$12; House Tour: Adults $27/$29, Children $22/$24; Infants (0-3) Free; $2 for EBT/SNAP recipients via Museums for All
highlights: Eight acres of historic gardens designed by landscape architect Ellen Biddle Shipman
Classical Revival mansion with original 1940s furnishings, artwork, and heirlooms
Interactive Discovery Garden designed for children of all ages
Edith & Edgar’s Garden Cafe featuring a plant-based menu with garden-sourced greens
Designated as a National Historic Landmark in 2005
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets in advance online for a discount; arrive at least 10 minutes early for scheduled tours; wear comfortable walking shoes to explore the extensive grounds

attractions.name: Louisiana Children's Museum
attractions.description: The Louisiana Children’s Museum is a 56,000-square-foot interactive educational resource located on 8.5 acres in New Orleans City Park. Recently relocated in 2019, it features indoor and outdoor exhibits focused on literacy, STEM, health, and environmental education. The museum includes age-appropriate zones from toddlers to tweens, a floating classroom, and restored lagoons
attractions.address: 15 Henry Thomas Drive, New Orleans, LA 70124
attractions.hours: Wednesday – Saturday: 9:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m., Sunday: 11:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m., Monday – Tuesday: Closed

highlights: Move With The River: A 100-foot-long interactive Mississippi River water exhibit
Follow That Food: An exhibit exploring New Orleans culture, food, and local spices
Outdoor Adventures: 8.5 acres of nature play, including a floating classroom and digging areas
Make Your Mark: A creative studio for art, design, and music
Play With Me: A dedicated brain-building gallery for children ages 0-3

attractions.nearby: New Orleans City Park, New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden, Botanical Garden, Carousel Gardens Amusement Park

attractions.name: Mid-City New Orleans
attractions.description: Mid-City is a vibrant, quirky, and unpretentious neighborhood in the heart of New Orleans, known for its historic shotgun houses and laid-back local atmosphere. It offers a more authentic, residential experience compared to the French Quarter, featuring a diverse array of local restaurants, bars, and iconic landmarks. The area is bisected by the historic Canal Street streetcar line and is bordered by the scenic Bayou St. John and City Park
attractions.address: Canal St & N Carrollton Ave, New Orleans, LA 70119
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Canal Street Streetcar: The iconic red streetcars run through the heart of the neighborhood, providing a scenic and historic transit option
City Park: One of the nation's oldest urban parks, home to the New Orleans Museum of Art and the Besthoff Sculpture Garden
Bayou St. John: A historic waterway popular for kayaking, paddleboarding, and waterside picnics
Lafitte Greenway: A 2.6-mile multi-use trail connecting Mid-City to the French Quarter, perfect for biking and walking
Historic Cemeteries: Famous above-ground burial sites like St. Louis Cemetery No. 3 and Greenwood Cemetery are located at the end of the streetcar line
Diverse Dining: Home to legendary spots like Parkway Bakery & Tavern and a rich variety of Vietnamese and Creole cuisine
attractions.tips: Take the red Canal Street streetcar for a leisurely tour of the neighborhood's historic homes and cemeteries. Rent a bike to explore the Lafitte Greenway, and be sure to stop for a roast beef po' boy at the famous Parkway Bakery & Tavern. If you're visiting in May, don't miss the Bayou Boogaloo festival along the banks of Bayou St. John
attractions.nearby: City Park, New Orleans Museum of Art, New Orleans Botanical Garden, Fair Grounds Race Course

attractions.name: New Canal Lighthouse Museum and Education Center
attractions.description: The New Canal Lighthouse Museum and Education Center is a reconstructed 1890s-era lighthouse that serves as a hub for coastal science and maritime history. Located on the southern shore of Lake Pontchartrain, it features interactive exhibits on the region's ecology, the history of its female lightkeepers, and ongoing coastal restoration efforts. The facility is operated by the Pontchartrain Conservancy and provides a unique educational experience combining historic preservation with hands-on environmental science
attractions.hours: Tuesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Saturday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Sunday: Closed; Monday: Closed
attractions.admission: $10.00 General Admission; $7.50 Seniors, Students, and Military; FREE for children under 6
highlights: Interactive 3D Watershed Sandbox for modeling stormwater flow
Help Grow Louisiana program for planting native seeds
Docent-guided tours covering lighthouse history and the Fresnel lens
Panoramic views of Lake Pontchartrain from the second-floor balcony
Underwater robotics (ROV) programs and water quality testing lab
On-site NOAA Tidal Gauge for real-time marine conditions
attractions.tips: The museum is fully accessible via an elevator lift for those with mobility needs. Visitors are encouraged to bring their own water samples for testing but should call ahead to prepare the lab. It is a family-friendly destination with educational activities suitable for children of all ages
attractions.nearby: New Orleans City Park, Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden, New Orleans Museum of Art, and waterfront restaurants such as The Blue Crab and Sala

attractions.name: New Orleans Botanical Garden
attractions.description: The New Orleans Botanical Garden is a 10-acre site in City Park containing more than 2,000 varieties of plants from around the world. Opened in 1936 as a WPA project, it is a rare example of public garden design from the Art Deco era, featuring the work of architect Richard Koch and sculptor Enrique Alférez. The garden offers a blend of natural landscape, historic architecture, and unique art installations
attractions.hours: Tuesday–Sunday: 10:00 AM – 4:30 PM (Last entry at 4:00 PM); Closed Mondays
attractions.admission: Adults: $15 for Louisiana residents, $25 for non-residents; Children (3–12): $7; Children (2 and under): Free; Free for Louisiana residents on Wednesdays
highlights: Helis Foundation Enrique Alférez Sculpture Garden with 15 sculptures set among oak trees
Conservatory of the Two Sisters featuring Living Fossils and Tropical Rainforest exhibits
Historic New Orleans Train Garden with 1,300 feet of track and replicas of city architecture
Yakumo Nihon Teien Japanese Garden showcasing traditional Japanese aesthetics
Lord & Taylor Rose Garden (The Parterre) with antique and modern rose varieties
attractions.tips: Bring a valid Louisiana ID to receive resident pricing; visit on Wednesdays for free admission if you are a Louisiana resident. Note that while the Train Garden is open during normal hours, the miniature trains only operate on Saturdays and Sundays
attractions.nearby: New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden, Storyland, Carousel Gardens Amusement Park, Louisiana Children's Museum

attractions.name: New Orleans City Park
attractions.description: New Orleans City Park is a massive 1,300-acre urban oasis featuring the world's largest collection of mature live oak trees, some over 800 years old. As the 'Heart of the City,' it offers a diverse blend of nature, culture, and recreation, including world-class museums, botanical gardens, and family-friendly amusement rides. It is one of the oldest and most visited urban parks in the United States, providing a serene escape just miles from the French Quarter.,)
attractions.hours: Park Grounds: 5am–10pm daily; Administrative Building: Mon–Thu 8am–5pm, Fri 8am–4pm; Botanical Garden & Storyland: Tue–Sun 10am–4:30pm; Carousel Gardens: Sat–Sun 11am–6pm; NOMA: Tue, Thu–Sun 10am–5pm, Wed 12pm–7pm (Closed Mon)

highlights: World's largest collection of mature live oak trees
Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden
New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA) and the New Orleans Botanical Garden
Carousel Gardens Amusement Park featuring a 1906 handcrafted carousel
Couturie Forest, home to the highest point of elevation in New Orleans
Recreational activities including boating, biking, tennis, and golf courses
attractions.tips: Visit the 'Singing Oak' to hear the wind chimes hidden in its branches, take advantage of free admission for Louisiana residents to the Botanical Garden on Wednesdays, and consider renting a swan paddleboat on Big Lake for a scenic view
attractions.nearby: St. Louis Cemetery No. 3, Bayou St. John, Fair Grounds Race Course, Pitot House, and the Mid-City neighborhood shops and restaurants

attractions.name: New Orleans Museum of Art
attractions.description: The New Orleans Museum of Art (NOMA), the city's oldest fine arts institution, houses an impressive permanent collection of nearly 50,000 artworks spanning 5,000 years. It is particularly noted for its strengths in French and American art, photography, and glass. The museum is set within the twelve-acre Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden in historic City Park

attractions.hours: Museum: Tue, Thu-Sun 10 am–5 pm, Wed 12 pm–7 pm, Mon Closed. Sculpture Garden: 10 am–6 pm (April–Sept), 10 am–5 pm (Oct–March)
attractions.admission: Louisiana Residents: Adults $18, Seniors (65+) $13, Active Military $13, University Students $11, Youth (19 & under) Free. Out-of-State Visitors: Adults $23, Seniors $18, Military $18, University Students $16. Sculpture Garden is Free
highlights: Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden with 90+ sculptures
Permanent collection of nearly 50,000 objects
Strong collection of French Impressionist works, including Edgar Degas
Extensive photography collection with over 12,000 works
On-site dining at Café NOMA by Ralph Brennan

attractions.name: Parkway Bakery and Tavern
attractions.description: Parkway Bakery and Tavern is a historic New Orleans landmark that has been a neighborhood icon since opening in 1911. Located in Mid-City near Bayou St. John, it is world-renowned for serving legendary "poor boy" sandwiches, particularly its slow-cooked roast beef with debris gravy. The family-owned establishment offers a vibrant atmosphere filled with local memorabilia and provides both indoor and outdoor covered seating
attractions.hours: Monday: Closed; Tuesday: Closed; Wednesday: 10:00 am – 6:00 pm; Thursday: 10:00 am – 6:00 pm; Friday: 10:00 am – 6:00 pm; Saturday: 10:00 am – 6:00 pm; Sunday: 10:00 am – 6:00 pm
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Iconic "poor boy" sandwiches served on fresh Leidenheimer French bread
Famous "Surf and Turf" po'boy featuring a combination of fried shrimp and roast beef with gravy
Slow-cooked roast beef prepared from a family recipe with over 50 gallons of gravy made weekly
Full-service tavern bar offering local cocktails and ice-cold Barq's root beer
Historic building decorated with over a century of New Orleans sports and culture memorabilia
Spacious outdoor covered patio with fans and heaters for year-round dining

The po'boys are very large and often shareable, and for the most authentic experience, order yours "dressed" with mayo, lettuce, pickles, and tomatoes. If the main line is too long, you can often find a seat at the bar and order your food there for a quicker experience

attractions.name: Pitot House
attractions.description: The Pitot House is a circa 1799 West Indies-style home located on Bayou St. John that served as the residence of James Pitot, the first mayor of New Orleans after its incorporation. It is the only Creole colonial country house open to the public in the city and is managed by the Louisiana Landmarks Society. The site features a restored 19th-century parterre garden and authentic period furnishings
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Friday: 10:00 a.m. – 3:00 p.m
attractions.admission: $15 regular admission; $12 students, seniors (65+), and National Trust members; Free for Louisiana Landmarks Society members
highlights: Only Creole colonial country house open to the public in New Orleans
Restored 19th-century parterre garden with magnolia and cypress trees
Former residence of James Pitot, New Orleans' first mayor after incorporation
Unique West Indies-style architecture with wide galleries and cooling brick floors
Historical connection to the movie Interview with a Vampire, which was filmed on-site
Saved from demolition in 1964 and moved 200 feet to its current location
attractions.tips: Tours are guided and typically last 45 minutes; it is recommended to call for availability if booking on the same day as your visit. The house is easily accessible via the Canal Streetcar (City Park/Museum line), ending at Beauregard Circle. Special group tours for six or more people may be arranged outside of regular hours by appointment

attractions.name: Pontchartrain Park Historic District
attractions.description: Pontchartrain Park is a historically significant neighborhood and public park in New Orleans, recognized as one of the first suburban-style subdivisions built specifically for African American middle-class families in the segregated South. Developed in the 1950s, the community features mid-century modern ranch-style homes centered around a sprawling public golf course and sports facilities. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2020 for its cultural and architectural importance
attractions.address: 6500 Press Drive, New Orleans, LA 70126 (Park & Stadium) | 6514 Congress Drive, New Orleans, LA 70126
attractions.hours: Park: Daily 6:00 AM – 10:00 PM; Golf Course: Tue–Fri 7:00 AM – 7:00 PM, Sat–Sun 6:30 AM – 7:00 PM (Closed Mondays)
attractions.admission: Park: Free; Golf: $30-$43 (18 holes), $30-$35 (9 holes), $15 (Juniors), $38 (Seniors)
highlights: Joseph M. Bartholomew, Sr. Municipal Golf Course, the first public course in New Orleans open to African Americans
Wesley Barrow Stadium, home to a Major League Baseball Urban Youth Academy
National Register of Historic Places designated district with significant mid-century architecture
Home to Southern University at New Orleans (SUNO), a prominent HBCU campus
Centrally located lagoons and walking paths designed with a 'Garden City' concept
attractions.tips: Book golf tee times in advance online to secure your preferred slot, especially on weekends. Visit Wesley Barrow Stadium during the spring to catch local youth or collegiate baseball games. attractions.description: Established in 1854, St. Louis Cemetery No. 3 is a historic Catholic cemetery known for its elaborate above-ground tombs and unique "City of the Dead" architecture (). It was built on the site of a former leper colony to accommodate the city's growth following the 1852 yellow fever epidemic (). The cemetery is the final resting place of many notable New Orleanians, including architects, musicians, and culinary icons like chef Leah Chase (,)

It features 18 larger-than-life storybook sculptures, including Captain Hook's pirate ship and the mouth of Pinocchio's whale, that children can climb, touch, and explore (). Founded in 2010 and housed in a converted residential building, it serves as a local favorite known for its friendly vibe and eclectic food pop-ups. The bar is famous for its unique signature drinks and welcoming environment that steers clear of the more tourist-heavy districts (,)

Originally developed in 1921 through land reclamation, it serves as a historic refuge featuring winding lagoons, scenic footbridges, and the remnants of the 1915 Darlington Electric Prismatic Fountain. The park offers a peaceful setting for walking, picnicking, and viewing the nearby marinas and Lake Pontchartrain
