attractions.hours: Box Office: Opens 1 hour prior to scheduled door times on show days only. Venue hours vary by event
attractions.days_open: Varies based on event schedule; Box Office is open on show days only
attractions.admission: Varies by show; typically range from $25-$100+. Customers can save $2.50-$6 per ticket by purchasing in person at the box office on show days
highlights: Iconic Art Deco architecture and historic neon marquee
1,200-capacity space with multi-tiered balcony seating
State-of-the-art audio and visual systems
Three permanent bars across a 10,000 square foot floor plan
Conveniently located at the intersection of Canal and Rampart Streetcar lines
attractions.tips: The Joy Theater is a 100% cashless venue for all concessions and box office purchases. Check individual event listings for age restrictions as they vary by show. For those with ADA needs, an elevator provides access to balcony seating
attractions.nearby: Saenger Theatre, Orpheum Theater, French Quarter, St. Louis Cemetery No. 1, Caesars Superdome

attractions.name: The Outlet Collection at Riverwalk
attractions.description: The Outlet Collection at Riverwalk is the nation's first upscale outlet center in a downtown setting, featuring over 75 retailers and restaurants along the Mississippi River. It offers a unique shopping and dining experience with significant discounts on major national brands like Nordstrom Rack, Coach, and Polo Ralph Lauren. The center is known for its vibrant waterfront atmosphere and scenic views of the river and passing ships
attractions.address: 500 Port of New Orleans Place, New Orleans, LA 70130
attractions.hours: Monday–Saturday: 10:00 AM – 7:00 PM; Sunday: 10:00 AM – 6:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Over 75 retailers and restaurants including Coach, Nordstrom Rack, and Polo Ralph Lauren Factory Store
Scenic waterfront views of the Mississippi River and the Crescent City Connection bridge
Nation's first upscale outlet center located in a major U.S. downtown setting
Home to a Cafe Du Monde location with shorter lines than the French Quarter original
Direct access to the Algiers Ferry and proximity to the New Orleans Morial Convention Center
Validation program for discounted parking at the adjacent Hilton "Whale" Lot with a $20 minimum purchase
attractions.tips: Visit the Cafe Du Monde inside the mall for shorter lines and air-conditioned seating. Bring your receipts to validation stations if you spend over $20 to receive a significant discount on parking at the 'Whale' Lot. The outdoor balcony on the upper levels offers excellent views of river traffic and the Crescent City Connection bridge. Be aware that the mall is a popular departure point for cruise passengers and can get very busy during embarkation times
attractions.nearby: Audubon Aquarium, Spanish Plaza, Woldenberg Park, Hilton New Orleans Riverside, New Orleans Morial Convention Center, Harrah's Casino New Orleans

attractions.name: The Polo Club Lounge at The Windsor Court
attractions.description: The Polo Club is a haven for both locals and visiting celebrities, reminiscent of a private English club decorated with dark woods and soft leather furnishings. It offers an ideal atmosphere for a pre-dinner cocktail or a nightcap, featuring one of the largest Cognac collections in the city. The lounge is also known for its nightly live jazz performances and upscale, comfortable ambiance
attractions.hours: Sunday – Thursday: 11:30 am – 11:00 pm; Friday & Saturday: 11:30 am – Midnight
attractions.admission: Free (entry); food and drink prices vary
highlights: Live local jazz nightly, including piano performances and weekend bands
One of the largest Cognac collections in New Orleans
Classic cocktail menu and elevated pub fare
Elegant English club-style décor with dark wood and leather furnishings
Located on the second floor of the luxurious Windsor Court Hotel
Home to nightly entertainment by local artists like John Royen and David Boeddinghaus
attractions.tips: Reservations are not required. Guests under 18 years of age are not permitted in the bar after 8:00 p.m., and pets are not allowed. Arrive early to secure a table for the live jazz performances, which typically start at 5:30 p.m
attractions.nearby: The Grill Room, Harrah's New Orleans Casino, Audubon Aquarium, The Sazerac House, Riverwalk Outlets, Canal Street

attractions.name: The Sazerac Bar at The Roosevelt New Orleans ()
attractions.description: The Sazerac Bar is a restored landmark that has kept cocktails and conversations flowing for decades in a timeless Art Deco setting. It offers a warm, welcoming space for gatherings where famous original cocktails provide a taste of true New Orleans hospitality (). The bar is renowned for its elegant African walnut bar and historic Paul Ninas murals ()
attractions.hours: Sunday – Thursday: 11:00 AM – 12:00 AM; Friday – Saturday: 11:00 AM – 1:00 AM ()
attractions.admission: Free ()
highlights: Home to the namesake Sazerac, often considered the world’s first mixed drink ()
Features a stunning African walnut bar and famous murals by local artist Paul Ninas ()
Renowned for its meticulously crafted Ramos Gin Fizz cocktails ()
Historic haunt of Louisiana governor Huey P. Long ()
Maintains an elegant Art Deco atmosphere reminiscent of the 1930s ()
attractions.tips: The bar can become very crowded, especially on weekends and during holidays; if full, guests can order drinks to enjoy in the hotel's grand lobby (). While the environment is upscale, the dress code is generally casual (). Don't forget to take in the Paul Ninas murals which are central to the bar's historic character ()
attractions.nearby: Saenger Theatre, Immaculate Conception Church, Canal Street Streetcars, French Quarter ()

attractions.name: Abita Brewing Company ()
attractions.description: Founded in 1986, Abita Brewing Company is Louisiana's first and largest craft brewery, known for using pure artesian spring water from the Southern Hills aquifer. The facility offers both guided and self-guided tours where visitors can learn about the brewing process and sample flagship favorites like Abita Amber and Purple Haze. It has grown from a local microbrewery into one of the most recognized craft beer brands in the Southeast (,)
attractions.hours: Taproom: Monday – Sunday, 11:00 AM – 9:00 PM; Guided Tours: Monday – Thursday at 1:00 PM & 3:00 PM, Friday – Sunday at 12:00 PM, 1:00 PM, 2:00 PM, & 3:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Guided Tours: $10 (includes four 4 oz. samples); Self-Guided Tours: Free; Children under 21: Free ()
highlights: Crafted with pure artesian spring water from local wells ()
State-of-the-art brewing facility with a recently expanded visitor center ()
Guided tours including samples and a souvenir glass or pint ()
Family-friendly environment offering world-famous Abita Root Beer ()
On-site Tap Room and Gift Shop with exclusive small-batch brews ()
Pet-friendly outdoor courtyard and beer garden ()
attractions.tips: Closed-toe shoes are mandatory for anyone participating in the guided brew house tour. Reservations are highly recommended for guided tours, though walk-ins are accommodated if space permits. The brewery is a cashless facility and only accepts credit or debit cards for tours and taproom purchases ()
attractions.nearby: Abita Brew Pub (original brewery site), Abita Mystery House (UCM Museum), Tammany Trace biking trail, Insta-Gator Ranch & Hatchery ()

attractions.name: Algiers Folk Art Zone & Blues Museum
attractions.description: Founded in 2000 by self-taught artist Charles Gillam, the Algiers Folk Art Zone & Blues Museum is a unique "living" folk art environment and museum. It features mixed-media statuary, sculptural forms, and music memorabilia that pay tribute to Louisiana's rich musical heritage, specifically the blues. The site serves as a studio, gallery, and community meeting spot for local and regional self-taught artists
attractions.hours: Monday–Sunday: By appointment only
attractions.admission: Varies / Fees may apply (Visiting is generally by appointment and often involves a donation or pre-arranged fee for guided tours)
highlights: A "living" folk art environment featuring works by founder Charles Gillam and other noted self-taught artists
The "Blues Greats" series, showcasing sculpted faces of legends like Al Hirt, Pete Fountain, and Memphis Minnie
Rare pieces by renowned folk artists including Roy Ferdinand, Lonnie Holley, and Mr. Imagination
Houses George Rodrigue's personal easel, a significant piece of Louisiana art history
Host of the annual Algiers Folk Art Festival, a fall event featuring live music, food, and regional art
attractions.tips: Contact founder Charles Gillam in advance via phone or email to schedule your tour, as the museum is not open for walk-ins. The best way to reach the site is by taking the scenic Algiers Ferry from the French Quarter/Canal Street terminal for just $2. Bring cash for the ferry and if you plan to purchase any of the affordable original artwork on-site
attractions.nearby: Algiers Ferry, Algiers Point Historic District, Old Point Bar, Crown and Anchor English Pub, Jazz Walk of Fame

attractions.name: Algiers Point
attractions.description: Algiers Point is a historic, village-like neighborhood located on the West Bank of the Mississippi River, directly across from the French Quarter. Established in 1719, it is the city's second-oldest neighborhood and is renowned for its beautifully preserved Victorian cottages, quiet residential streets, and lush greenery. Its unique position offers some of the most spectacular panoramic views of the New Orleans skyline and the river's "Crescent" bend
attractions.address: Bounded by the Mississippi River to the north and west, Atlantic Avenue to the east, and Newton Street to the south (Representative Address: Algiers Ferry Terminal, 138 Morgan St, New Orleans, LA 70114)
attractions.hours: Neighborhood: Always open; Algiers Ferry: Daily from 6:00 AM to 12:15 AM (Departs Algiers on the hour and half-hour; departs Canal Street at :15 and :45 past the hour)
attractions.admission: Free to explore the neighborhood; Algiers Ferry costs $2.00 for adults, $1.00 for seniors (65+) and youth (5-18), and is free for children under 2
highlights: Historic Algiers Ferry ride across the Mississippi River
Jazz Walk of Fame along the levee featuring statues of musical legends
Well-preserved 19th-century architecture, including Victorian and Creole cottages
Confetti Park, a whimsical community-built playground
Old Point Bar and Crown & Anchor English Pub for local atmosphere and live music
Breathtaking skyline views of the French Quarter and CBD from the levee
attractions.tips: The best way to arrive is by the Canal Street Ferry for just $2; the views are better than many expensive river cruises. Visit the levee during 'golden hour' for the city's best sunset photos and skyline reflections. Use the 'Le Pass' app to purchase ferry tickets in advance to avoid needing exact change
attractions.nearby: French Quarter (via ferry), Audubon Aquarium and Insectarium, Vue Orleans observation deck, Canal Street shopping, Old Algiers Folk Art and Blues Museum

attractions.name: Atchafalaya Basin Landing & Swamp Tours
attractions.description: Atchafalaya Basin Landing & Swamp Tours is a family-owned destination providing immersive experiences in America's largest freshwater swamp. Since 1999, they have offered educational airboat and barge tours highlighting the region's unique wildlife and Cajun culture. Visitors can explore narrow passages and open lake areas while learning about the vibrant ecosystem from local guides
attractions.hours: Daily: 7:00 AM – 5:00 PM; General tour times: 9:00 AM, 11:00 AM, 1:00 PM, and 3:00 PM
attractions.admission: Airboat Tour: $65 Adults, $39 Children (10 & under); Traditional Barge Tour: $39 Adults, $25 Children (10 & under); Private Tours: $650
highlights: Airboat tours through America's largest freshwater swamp
Close encounters with alligators including famous residents like Peanut and Hercules
Wildlife viewing of various bird species, turtles, and lush cypress forests
On-site Turtle's Bar featuring a large covered patio and live music events
Handicap-accessible facilities and family-friendly atmosphere
attractions.tips: Wear sunscreen and bug repellent; bring your own food and water if desired; arrive early for reservations and use the restroom before the 1.5-hour tour; hearing protection and life vests are provided for airboat tours
attractions.nearby: Buck & Johnny's (Breaux Bridge), Billy's Boudin and Cracklin (Lafayette), McGee's Swamp Tours, Cypress Cove Landing

attractions.name: Audubon Louisiana Nature Center
attractions.description: The Audubon Louisiana Nature Center is a 86-acre environmental education center nestled in New Orleans East. It features a lush bottomland hardwood forest with a network of trails, a state-of-the-art planetarium, and an interpretive center filled with interactive exhibits. The center serves as a vital community resource for exploring the unique ecosystems of South Louisiana
attractions.address: 11000 Lake Forest Blvd, New Orleans, LA 70128-2816
attractions.hours: Nature Center: Wed-Sun 8:30 AM – 3:30 PM; Restrooms, Boardwalk & Trails: Wed-Sun 8:30 AM – 3:00 PM; Interpretive Center: Thu-Sun 10:00 AM – 2:00 PM; Planetarium: Sat-Sun (Check showtimes)
attractions.admission: Nature Center trails and exhibits: Free; Planetarium shows: $8 for adults (13-64), $6 for children (2-12), and $6 for seniors (65+). Membership discounts may apply
highlights: Scenic Nature Trails
Audubon Planetarium with immersive shows
Interpretive Center with live animals and biofacts
Educational Programs like 'Nature Neighbors' on Saturdays
Wildlife viewing areas in a 86-acre hardwood forest
Free on-site parking and accessible boardwalks
attractions.tips: The outdoor trails are free, but planetarium shows require a separate ticket. Bring bug spray and water as the trails are in a forested wetland environment. Visit on Saturdays at 11:00 AM for the free 'Nature Neighbors' public programs
attractions.nearby: Joe Brown Park, Cajun Fire Brewing Company, Lakefront Arena, Villalobos Rescue Center

attractions.name: Chalmette Battlefield - Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve
attractions.description: Chalmette Battlefield is the historic site of the 1815 Battle of New Orleans, the final major engagement of the War of 1812. The park preserves the grounds where General Andrew Jackson's forces defeated a British invasion, featuring a 100-foot-high monument and a reconstructed American rampart. Visitors can explore various layers of history, including the antebellum Malus-Beauregard House and the adjacent National Cemetery (,)
attractions.hours: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM (Vehicular gates 9:00 AM - 4:30 PM; site open to pedestrians sunrise to sunset) ()
attractions.days_open: Daily; closed on most federal holidays including New Year's Day, MLK Day, Washington's Birthday, Juneteenth, Independence Day, Labor Day, Columbus Day, Thanksgiving, and Christmas Day ()
attractions.admission: Free (,)
highlights: Chalmette Monument (100-foot obelisk) ()
Malus-Beauregard House (1830s plantation home) ()
Reconstructed American Rampart ()
Chalmette National Cemetery ()
Visitor Center with films and exhibits ()
Daily Ranger-led talks and demonstrations ()
attractions.tips: Arrive via the Creole Queen paddlewheeler from the French Quarter for a scenic approach. Climb the 122 steps of the Chalmette Monument for panoramic views of the battlefield and the Mississippi River. Wear comfortable walking shoes for the self-guided trails and check the visitor center schedule for daily ranger talks (,)
attractions.nearby: Chalmette National Cemetery, Malus-Beauregard House, Rocky and Carlos Restaurant, Mississippi River (,)

attractions.name: Chalmette National Cemetery
attractions.description: Established in 1864, Chalmette National Cemetery serves as the final resting place for more than 15,000 veterans from the War of 1812 to the Vietnam War. It is uniquely located on the site of the 1815 Battle of New Orleans, adjacent to the Chalmette Battlefield. The cemetery is one of only 14 national cemeteries managed by the National Park Service
attractions.address: 8606 West St. Bernard Highway, Chalmette, LA 70043
attractions.hours: Vehicular Gates: Monday-Sunday 9:30 am – 4:30 pm (including federal holidays); Pedestrian Access: Monday-Sunday Sunrise – Sunset
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Over 15,000 headstones of veterans from multiple American conflicts
Grave of Sarah Rosetta Wakeman, a woman who fought in the Civil War disguised as a man
The 100-foot-high Chalmette Monument obelisk
Historic Malus-Beauregard House located on the grounds
Reconstructed American rampart from the Battle of New Orleans
Self-guided audio tour sharing stories of those buried on site
attractions.tips: Take the self-guided audio tour by calling 504-799-0803 to hear stories of the fallen. Climbing the 122 steps of the Chalmette Monument offers panoramic views of the battlefield. Please note that metal detectors are strictly prohibited on these federal grounds. For a scenic arrival, consider the Creole Queen riverboat cruise from the French Quarter
attractions.nearby: Chalmette Battlefield, Jackson Barracks, French Quarter, Creole Queen Riverboat

attractions.name: Destrehan Plantation
attractions.description: Established in 1787, Destrehan Plantation is the oldest documented plantation home in the lower Mississippi Valley and the nearest to New Orleans. The site features a grand French Colonial-style home and serves as a living history museum with guided tours led by costumed interpreters. Visitors can explore the complex history of the Destrehan family and the lives of the enslaved people who sustained the plantation through detailed exhibits and original structures (,)
attractions.hours: Daily: 9:00 AM – 5:30 PM; First tour starts at 9:30 AM, with tours running every half hour (or hour depending on season) until the final tour at 4:00 PM ()
attractions.days_open: Daily, except for major holidays including New Year's Day, Mardi Gras Day, Easter Sunday, Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Eve, and Christmas Day ()
attractions.admission: Adults (18-59): $27; Seniors (60+): $25; Children (7-17): $16; Active Military: $24; Private Tours: $38 per person (min 4 people); Group Tours (20+): $19 per person ()
highlights: Oldest documented plantation home in the lower Mississippi Valley, dating back to 1787 ()
The 1811 German Coast Uprising exhibit, detailing the largest slave revolt in U.S. history ()
The Jefferson Room, featuring an original document signed by Thomas Jefferson and James Madison ()
Rost Home Colony exhibit, which tells the story of formerly enslaved people under the Freedmen’s Bureau ()
Original enslaved cabins relocated from Helvetia Plantation to preserve their history ()
Guided tours led by costumed historical interpreters who share stories of both free and enslaved residents ()
attractions.tips: Free on-site parking and luggage storage are provided for visitors, which is convenient for those traveling to or from the nearby airport. Photography is permitted on the grounds and inside the house as long as it does not delay the tours, though video and audio recording are prohibited during guided sessions. Visitors should allow at least 1 to 2 hours for the full experience, including self-guided exploration of the dependency buildings and museums (,)
attractions.nearby: Ormond Plantation, Mississippi River, Louis Armstrong New Orleans International Airport, German Coast Farmers Market (,)

attractions.name: Dr. Wagner's Honey Island Swamp Tours
attractions.description: Dr. Wagner's Honey Island Swamp Tours offers an authentic, two-hour narrated journey through one of the most pristine river swamps in the United States. Led by native professional guides, visitors explore the protected wetlands of Honey Island to observe alligators, bald eagles, and other diverse wildlife in their natural habitat. The tour provides a non-commercialized look at Louisiana's unique swamp ecology and natural history
attractions.hours: Monday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Tuesday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Wednesday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Thursday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Friday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Saturday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM, Sunday: 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM
attractions.admission: Boat Tour: $34.99 for adults, $23.99 for children; Tour with Roundtrip Transportation from New Orleans: $64.99 for adults, $43.99 for children
highlights: Two-hour professionally narrated boat tours led by native Louisiana guides
Frequent sightings of alligators, turtles, herons, and wild hogs in a protected nature reserve
Access to deep swamp interiors and shallow backwaters via specialized Coast Guard-inspected boats
Educational insights into Cajun history, swamp legends, and local ecology
On-site gift shop offering snacks, drinks, and souvenirs overlooking the swamp
attractions.tips: Reservations are required for all tours. Visitors should arrive 30 minutes prior to departure and dress appropriately for the weather. Note that the on-site gift shop may only accept cash for smaller purchases
attractions.nearby: Olde Towne Slidell, Slidell Museum, Heritage Park, Bogue Chitto National Wildlife Refuge, and INFINITY Science Center

attractions.name: Global Wildlife Center
attractions.description: Global Wildlife Center is one of the largest free-roaming wildlife preserves of its kind in the United States, spanning 900 acres of Louisiana countryside. The center is home to over 2,000 exotic, endangered, and threatened animals from around the world that roam freely across the property. Visitors can experience guided safari tours to interact with and feed animals like giraffes, zebras, and camels in a serene, predator-free environment
attractions.hours: Monday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Tuesday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Wednesday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Thursday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Friday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Saturday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Sunday: 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM. (Tour hours may vary by season; booking in advance is recommended)
attractions.admission: Standard Wagon Tour: Adults (12-61) $21, Seniors (62+) $19, Children (2-11) $15, Children under 2 Free. Private Tours: $49.95 per person (minimum 4 people or $199.80). Animal Feed: Souvenir Cup $5, Family Bucket $35, Sack $71, Bin $82
highlights: Guided Safari Wagon Tours across 900 acres of free-roaming habitat
Interactive animal feeding opportunities for species like reticulated giraffes and Bactrian camels
Private Pinzgauer Tours for a more intimate and off-road safari experience
Home to over 2,000 exotic and endangered animals including American bison and Grant's zebras
Educational field trips and special encounters like 'Capy Creativity' with capybaras
Membership options for unlimited tours and conservation support
attractions.tips: Buy animal feed (cups or buckets) at the start of your tour to enhance the experience, as most animals will come right up to the wagon for food. Wear comfortable clothing and sunscreen as the wagons are open-sided, and bring a camera for close-up wildlife shots. Private tours are recommended for those wanting to interact specifically with giraffes, as they may stay further from the larger wagons
attractions.nearby: Sunflower Farm and Ranch, Far Horizons Art Gallery, Bogue Chitto State Park, and Honey Island Swamp

attractions.name: Grand Isle State Park
attractions.description: Grand Isle State Park is a 140-acre preserve situated at the eastern tip of Louisiana's only inhabited barrier island. It serves as a critical breakwater between the Gulf of Mexico and inland channels, offering the state's only state-owned and operated beach. The park is a premier destination for world-class salt-water fishing, birding, and coastal camping (,))
attractions.address: 108 Admiral Craik Drive, Grand Isle, LA 70358
attractions.hours: Gate: 7:00 AM - 9:00 PM daily; Entrance Station: Sun-Thu 8:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Fri-Sat 8:00 AM - 8:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: $3 per person; Free for seniors (62 & older); Free for children (3 & under) ()
highlights: Louisiana's only state-owned and operated Gulf Coast beach
400-foot fishing pier extending into the Gulf of Mexico
2.5-mile nature trail ideal for birdwatching along the Mississippi Flyway
Observation tower providing panoramic views of the island and Gulf
Beachfront camping with both RV and tent sites available
Home to the historic Grand Isle International Tarpon Rodeo every July
attractions.tips: A toll is required to cross the bridge when traveling southbound to the island. Visitors should shuffle their feet in the water to avoid stingrays and note that glass containers and dogs are prohibited on the designated swimming beach ()
attractions.nearby: Town of Grand Isle, Barataria Bay, deep-sea fishing charters, Grand Isle Migratory Bird Celebration ()

attractions.name: Honey Island Swamp
attractions.description: Honey Island Swamp is one of the most pristine and least-altered river swamps in the United States, covering nearly 70,000 acres of protected wetlands. Visitors can explore the 250-square-mile ecosystem via narrated boat tours that navigate through moss-draped cypress trees and winding bayous. The area is famous for its diverse wildlife and local legends, including the elusive Honey Island Swamp Monster
attractions.hours: Daily departures at 9:30 AM, 12:15 PM, 2:45 PM, and 5:00 PM; Office open 7:30 AM – 9:00 PM
attractions.admission: Boat Tour (Drive-out): $34.99 Adults, $23.99 Children (ages 3-12); With New Orleans Hotel Pickup: $64.99 Adults, $43.99 Children
highlights: Guided tours through one of America's most pristine swamp ecosystems
Frequent sightings of alligators, bald eagles, wild boars, and herons
Moss-draped ancient cypress trees and lush wetland flora
Narration on Cajun culture, swamp ecology, and local Bigfoot lore
Custom-built boats designed for deep access into shallow backwaters
Excellent opportunities for nature and wildlife photography
attractions.tips: Reservations are required for all tours. It is recommended to arrive at least 30 minutes before departure for check-in. Morning tours are often preferred during warmer months to avoid peak afternoon heat, and visitors should dress for the weather as boats are open-air
attractions.nearby: Olde Towne Slidell, INFINITY Science Center, Camp Salmen Nature Park, Fontainebleau State Park, Palmettos on the Bayou

attractions.name: Houmas House Plantation and Gardens
attractions.description: Houmas House Plantation and Gardens is a historic estate and museum that allows visitors to experience the opulent life of a wealthy sugarcane plantation owner in the 1800s. The mansion, known as the 'Sugar Palace,' has been meticulously restored to its antebellum grandeur and is surrounded by 38 acres of lush, formal gardens. It serves as both a private residence and a major tourist destination along Louisiana's historic River Road (,)
attractions.hours: Monday - Sunday: 9:00 AM – 7:00 PM ()
attractions.days_open: Open 7 days a week (Closed Christmas Day and New Year’s Day) ()
attractions.admission: Mansion and Gardens Tour: Adults (18+) $35, Teenagers (13-17) $25, Children (6-12) $15, Young Children (5 & under) Free; Self-Guided Garden Tour: Adults (18+) $20
highlights: 38 acres of meticulously landscaped formal gardens and sitting nooks
Guided mansion tours led by history-narrating guides in period dress
The Great River Road Museum, exploring life along the Mississippi River
Three on-site restaurants featuring world-class Louisiana Creole cuisine
The Inn at Houmas House, offering overnight stays in historic-style cottages
Ancient oak alley and Greek Revival architecture of the main house
attractions.tips: Wear comfortable walking shoes as the estate grounds are extensive; high heels are not recommended. Be aware that Uber and taxi services are generally unavailable from New Orleans or Baton Rouge, so consider booking a tour that includes transportation if you do not have a car ()
attractions.nearby: The Great River Road Museum, LSU Rural Life Museum, Tanger Outlet Mall, Louisiana's Old State Capitol, and other historic plantations ()

attractions.name: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve, Barataria Preserve
attractions.description: The Barataria Preserve encompasses over 26,000 acres of wild Louisiana wetlands, including hardwood forests, swamps, and marshes (). Visitors can explore these ecosystems via boardwalks and waterways to spot alligators, turtles, and over 200 species of birds (). It is one of six sites that make up the Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve ()
attractions.hours: Trails (Twin Canals): Open daily (day-use only); Visitor Center: Closed for construction (as of Feb 2026); Staff availability at Wetland Trace (4917 City Park Drive): Wed-Thu 10:00 AM–2:00 PM ()
attractions.admission: Free (,)
highlights: 26,000 acres of diverse wetlands including cypress swamps and marshes ()
Extensive wooden boardwalk trails such as the Palmetto and Bayou Coquille trails ()
Abundant wildlife viewing opportunities including American alligators and migratory birds ()
Canoe and kayak launch access at the Twin Canals area ()
Ranger-led programs and guided wetland walks ()
attractions.tips: Check the official website for current trail closures due to construction; bring insect repellent for mosquitoes, and always maintain a safe distance from wildlife like alligators (,)
attractions.nearby: Lafitte Barataria Museum, Restaurant des Familles, Bayou Segnette State Park, and the French Quarter Visitor Center (,)

attractions.name: Jean Lafitte Swamp Tours
attractions.description: Jean Lafitte Swamp Tours provides an authentic Cajun-style boat journey through the heart of Southern Louisiana's swamplands within the protected Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve's Barataria Preserve. Visitors explore meandering bayous aboard Coast Guard-certified boats, encountering moss-draped cypress trees and a variety of wildlife such as alligators, egrets, and nutria. Native Cajun guides enrich the experience with local legends and lore during the approximately 1 hour 45-minute tour
attractions.address: 6601 Leo Kerner Lafitte Parkway, Marrero, LA 70072
attractions.hours: Office: 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM Daily; Tour Times: 10:00 AM, 12:00 PM, 2:00 PM, and seasonal 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adult Drive-up: $32; Adult with Transportation: $62; Child (4-12) Drive-up: $12; Child (4-12) with Transportation: $42; Child (0-3): Free; Child (0-3) with Transportation: $30
highlights: Guided tours through the protected Barataria Preserve in Jean Lafitte National Historical Park
Frequent sightings of wildlife including alligators, snakes, turtles, and diverse bird species
Native Cajun guides providing local history and ecological insights
Coast Guard-certified boats equipped with restroom facilities
Handicap accessible tours for both standard and airboat options
Specialized seasonal experiences like the Princess Tiana Swamp Experience
attractions.tips: Make reservations at least 24-48 hours in advance as tours are popular and fill up quickly. Bring cash for tipping guides and purchasing snacks or souvenirs on-site. Dress in comfortable, seasonal layers and bring sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses, hat) and a camera for wildlife photography
attractions.nearby: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve - Barataria Preserve (Visitor Center & Trails), Restaurant Des Familles, Voleo's Seafood Restaurant

attractions.name: Jungle Gardens
attractions.category: Park, Botanical Garden, and Bird Sanctuary
attractions.description: Jungle Gardens is a 170-acre semitropical garden and bird sanctuary founded by Edward Avery McIlhenny on Avery Island. The site features a three-mile self-guided trail through marshes and bayous, showcasing live oaks, exotic plants, and diverse wildlife. It is home to the famous Bird City rookery and a centuries-old Buddha statue

attractions.days_open: Open 365 days a year, including all holidays

attractions.name: Laura Plantation: Louisiana's Creole Heritage Site
attractions.description: Laura Plantation is a historic Louisiana Creole heritage site that offers an intimate look into the lives of four generations of a single Creole family and their enslaved people. The guided tour is uniquely based on the memoirs of Laura Locoul Gore, providing a firsthand account of plantation life from the early 19th century through the post-Civil War era. Visitors can explore the colorful Creole "Big House," authentic slave cabins, and lush gardens while learning about the complex cultural intersection of French, West-African, and American history. ()
attractions.hours: Tours depart daily every 40 minutes. First tour: 10:00 AM; Last tour: 3:20 PM. Closed: New Year's Day, Mardi Gras, Easter Sunday, Thanksgiving, and Christmas. (Last tour at 12:00 PM on July 4 and Dec 24) ()
attractions.admission: Adult (18+): $28, Teen (13-17): $20, Child (6-12): $12, Infants (5 and under): Free with paying adult ()
highlights: Guided tour based on the memoirs of Laura Locoul Gore and archival documents ()
Visit to the 1805 Creole "Big House" with its raised basement and galleries ()
Exploration of 200-year-old sugar plantation homestead and three historic gardens ()
Authentic 1840s slave cabins where the "Compair Lapin" (Br'er Rabbit) tales were recorded ()
Museum exhibit "From the Big House to the Quarters: Slavery on Laura Plantation" ()
History of the site's role in the U.S. Colored Troops during the Civil War ()
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended to guarantee a tour spot. Wear comfortable walking shoes as the 75-minute tour involves significant walking through the house and grounds. Arrive at least 15-20 minutes before your scheduled tour to check in. ()
attractions.nearby: Oak Alley Plantation, Whitney Plantation, Evergreen Plantation, St. Joseph Plantation, Destrehan Plantation ()

attractions.name: Louisiana State Capitol
attractions.description: The Louisiana State Capitol is the seat of government for the state of Louisiana and is the tallest capitol building in the United States, standing at 450 feet. This Art Deco skyscraper was completed in 1932 under the direction of Governor Huey P. Long and features a grand Memorial Hall and an observation deck with panoramic views. It is a National Historic Landmark that symbolizes Louisiana's political history and architectural ambition
attractions.address: 900 North Third Street, Baton Rouge, LA 70802
attractions.hours: 8:00 a.m. - 4:30 p.m. daily
attractions.days_open: Open Daily except for New Year's Day, Easter Sunday, Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Eve, and Christmas Day
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Tallest state capitol building in the United States at 450 feet tall
Observation deck on the 27th floor offering views of Baton Rouge and the Mississippi River
Stunning Art Deco architecture and interior design, including the 124-foot-long Memorial Hall
Site of the 1935 assassination of Governor Huey P. Long, with bullet holes still visible in the marble walls
Beautifully landscaped 27-acre Capitol Gardens with paths lined by boxwood hedges
attractions.tips: Check the current status of the observation deck before visiting, as it may be closed for renovations. Take the elevator to the 27th floor for the best views, and don't miss the basement to see the historic bullet holes. Self-guided tours are available, and the welcome center staff can provide local maps and brochures
attractions.nearby: Old State Capitol, Old Governor's Mansion, Capitol Park Museum, USS Kidd, Louisiana Art and Science Museum, Old Arsenal Museum

attractions.name: Louisiana's Old State Capitol
attractions.description: Louisiana's Old State Capitol, also known as the "Castle on the River," is a stunning Gothic Revival building that served as the state's seat of government from 1847 until 1932. Today, it functions as a museum of political history, featuring award-winning interactive exhibits and a world-class multimedia presentation. The interior is renowned for its magnificent stained glass dome and its commanding cast-iron spiral staircase ()
attractions.hours: Tuesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Saturday: 9:00 AM - 3:00 PM, Sunday: Closed, Monday: Closed ()
attractions.admission: Free (Museum and exhibits); $3 per person for "The Ghost of the Castle" multimedia show ()
highlights: Breathtaking stained glass cathedral dome and skylight
Commanding cast-iron spiral staircase and marble-decorated interior
"The Ghost of the Castle" award-winning multimedia history presentation
Extensive exhibits on the life and assassination of Huey Long
Unique Gothic Revival architecture resembling a medieval fortress
Prime location on a bluff overlooking the Mississippi River
attractions.tips: Admission to the museum is free, but the $3 Ghost of the Castle show is highly recommended for a colorful history of the building. For the best photos of the stained glass dome, try to visit around 11:00 AM or 3:00 PM when the sunlight hits the glass most directly. Metered street parking is available nearby, and the building is handicap accessible via the back entrance on St. Philip Street (,)
attractions.nearby: USS Kidd Veterans Museum, Louisiana Art & Science Museum, LSU Museum of Art, Capitol Park Museum, Repentance Park ()

attractions.name: Middendorf's Seafood Restaurant Manchac
attractions.description: Middendorf's is a historic Louisiana seafood institution established in 1934, famously known as the home of the "World Famous Original Thin Fried Catfish." Situated in the fishing village of Manchac between Lakes Maurepas and Pontchartrain, the restaurant offers a waterfront dining experience with deep roots in local tradition. It has grown from a small family eatery into a large-scale destination that serves thousands of customers weekly with its signature crispy catfish and fresh Gulf seafood
attractions.hours: Wednesday: 10:30am-9:00pm; Thursday: 10:30am-9:00pm; Friday: 10:30am-9:00pm; Saturday: 10:30am-9:00pm; Sunday: 10:30am-9:00pm; Monday-Tuesday: Closed
attractions.admission: Free entry to restaurant; menu prices vary (e.g., thin catfish platters ~$19+)

Annual special events including Oktoberfest with authentic German cuisine and Fettuccine Frenzy
Family-friendly amenities including a sand play area and a mini animal enclosure with chickens and geese
Accessible by boat with dedicated docking space for diners
attractions.tips: The restaurant does not take reservations and operates on a first-come, first-served basis, so expect crowds on weekends. If the weather is nice, ask for a table on the outdoor patio to enjoy better views of the water. Don't miss the seasonal specialties like the house-made turtle soup or boiled Manchac crabs when in season

attractions.name: Natchitoches National Historic Landmark District
attractions.description: Established in 1714, the Natchitoches Historic District is the oldest permanent settlement in the Louisiana Purchase territory. This 33-block National Historic Landmark District features charming wrought-iron balconies, brick-paved streets, and a wealth of 18th and 19th-century architecture overlooking Cane River Lake. It serves as the cultural heart of the region, hosting vibrant festivals and offering a blend of French colonial heritage and Southern hospitality
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (District); Individual attraction and shop hours typically 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free (District); Museum and tour prices vary

Filming location for the classic movie 'Steel Magnolias'

attractions.name: New Orleans Baby Cakes
attractions.description: The New Orleans Baby Cakes were a Triple-A Minor League Baseball team and affiliate of the Miami Marlins that competed in the Pacific Coast League. Based at the Shrine on Airline in Metairie, Louisiana, the team was celebrated for its uniquely NOLA-themed branding and family-friendly atmosphere. Although the franchise moved to Wichita in 2019, the stadium remains a prominent local venue for sports and community events

attractions.name: Nottoway Plantation & Resort
attractions.description: Nottoway is a historic plantation complex near White Castle, Louisiana, originally featuring the largest antebellum mansion in the Southern United States. Following a devastating fire in May 2025 that destroyed the main house, the site is currently undergoing a multi-year reconstruction to build an exact replica. Parts of the property, including the grounds, restaurant, and several historic outbuildings, remain open to visitors during the rebuilding process

Once a sugar plantation, it now serves as a museum dedicated to preserving and interpreting its complex history, including the lives of the enslaved people who built and maintained it. Visitors can explore the Greek Revival mansion, restored slave cabins, and extensive gardens (,)

Recently renovated, the facility features modernized locker rooms, elite weight rooms, and dedicated player areas designed to support professional athletic performance. The site also includes administrative offices, a multi-media studio, and a 75,000 square-foot indoor practice arena

attractions.name: Pearl River Wildlife Management Area
attractions.description: The Pearl River Wildlife Management Area is a 35,619-acre protected tract known for its diverse ecosystems, including bottomland hardwoods and the pristine Honey Island Swamp. Managed by the Louisiana Department of Wildlife and Fisheries, it provides a vital habitat for species like alligators, bald eagles, and black bears. Visitors can enjoy a wide range of outdoor activities such as hunting, fishing, paddling, and birding within its scenic bayous and forests
attractions.hours: WMA Grounds: Daily, 4:00 AM – 2 hours after sunset; Honey Island Shooting Range: Friday – Sunday, 7:30 AM – 4:30 PM
attractions.days_open: Open daily year-round, subject to flooding closures

Over 35,000 acres of protected hardwood forest and cypress-tupelo swamp

Diverse wildlife viewing including alligators, deer, and rare bird species

attractions.name: St. Bernard Parish
attractions.description: St. Bernard Parish is a historic coastal community located just five miles from New Orleans' French Quarter, offering a unique blend of Spanish heritage and world-class outdoor adventure. It is famously home to the Chalmette Battlefield, the site of the 1815 Battle of New Orleans, and serves as a gateway to Louisiana's bountiful fishing and birding wetlands. Visitors can explore the San Bernardo Scenic Byway, which connects historic districts, plantation ruins, and the last living vestige of Spanish colonial Louisiana
attractions.hours: Visitor Center: Monday 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Tuesday 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Wednesday 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Thursday 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Friday 8:30 AM - 4:30 PM, Saturday Closed, Sunday Closed
attractions.days_open: Visitor Center: Monday - Friday; Chalmette Battlefield: Daily

highlights: Chalmette Battlefield & National Cemetery, the site of the Battle of New Orleans
Los Isleños Museum & Village, preserving Canary Island Spanish culture
World-class fishing charters in Delacroix Island and Shell Beach
The San Bernardo Scenic Byway featuring historic live oaks and plantation ruins
Old Arabi Cultural District, known for its historic architecture and arts
St. Bernard State Park, offering camping and nature trails along the Mississippi River
attractions.tips: Be sure to book fishing charters in advance, as the parish is a premier destination for redfish and speckled trout. Don't miss the Los Isleños Fiesta in March for a deep dive into local Spanish heritage and food. Try the legendary po-boys and local seafood at Rocky & Carlos in Chalmette

Visitors can explore a self-guided experience covering the brand's history and production process, from the pepper greenhouse to the barrel aging warehouse and bottling line. The island also features the 170-acre Jungle Gardens, a semitropical nature preserve and bird sanctuary

Pepper Greenhouse with diverse pepper varieties

Blending Room and active Bottling Line viewing area
Jungle Gardens and Bird City wildfowl refuge

attractions.name: The Cajun Village & Coffee House
attractions.description: The Cajun Village is a charming collection of authentic, restored Acadian buildings dating back to the late 19th and early 20th centuries, now serving as a unique shopping and dining destination in Sorrento. Visitors can explore various specialty shops offering Louisiana pottery, antiques, and local crafts, or relax at The Coffee House with classic beignets and cafe au lait. The site also features a resident alligator pond, providing a peaceful and historic glimpse into Louisiana's Cajun heritage. (,)

attractions.name: USS Kidd Veterans Museum
attractions.description: The USS Kidd Veterans Museum is a premier military history destination centered around the Fletcher-class destroyer USS KIDD (DD-661), the only ship of its kind restored to its 1945 World War II configuration. Located on the Mississippi River, the site features a shoreside museum with extensive exhibits on naval history, including a P-40 aircraft and the Louisiana Veterans Hall of Honor. It serves as a profound memorial to the men and women of the American armed forces, offering a unique glimpse into the life of a WWII sailor

attractions.description: Vermilionville is a 23-acre living history museum and folklife park located on the banks of the Bayou Vermilion that preserves and represents the Acadian, Creole, and Native American cultures from 1765 to 1890. The village features 19 attractions, including seven restored original homes where costumed artisans demonstrate traditional crafts such as spinning and woodcarving. It serves as a premiere cultural destination in Lafayette, offering a mix of history, music, and authentic Cajun and Creole cuisine

attractions.name: Mardi Gras New Orleans
attractions.description: Mardi Gras is New Orleans' premier cultural celebration, a season of spectacular parades, vibrant costumes, and historic traditions culminating on Fat Tuesday. The festivities feature elaborate floats organized by social clubs known as krewes, who toss "throws" like beads and trinkets to massive crowds. It is a city-wide event that blends European carnival customs with unique Louisiana heritage
